title: Verify SSH from MacBook to all four hosts
steps: ✓ Complete — onboarding is done: the secrets vault, SSH aliases, sudo, and the agent-session flow are all live. This checklist item was folded into the staged Runs 0–7; kept for history.
From your MacBook (operator station), confirm `~/.ssh/config` has aliases from `configs/network/ssh-config.example` — `nas`, `mini`, `rig`, `seedbox`.
Test each host: `ssh nas 'hostname'`, `ssh mini 'hostname'`, `ssh seedbox 'hostname'`.
For the rig (24/7): `ssh rig 'hostname'` — if it's down (power outage / accidental shutdown), recover with `RIG_MAC=<mac> ./scripts/gaming/wake-rig.sh && sleep 30`.
If any host fails, fix keys or Tailscale before starting agent work — see `configs/network/ssh-maintenance-access.md`.
commands: ssh rig 'hostname'  # if down, recover: RIG_MAC=<mac> ./scripts/gaming/wake-rig.sh
verify: All four aliases return a hostname without password prompts (Tailscale SSH or homelab key).

title: Create local secrets vault from template
steps: ✓ Complete — onboarding is done: the secrets vault, SSH aliases, sudo, and the agent-session flow are all live. This checklist item was folded into the staged Runs 0–7; kept for history.
In `foss-setup/`: `cp .handoff-secrets.yaml.example .handoff-secrets.yaml`
Restrict permissions: `chmod 600 .handoff-secrets.yaml`
Confirm `.handoff-secrets.yaml` is gitignored (repo root `.gitignore`).
You will fill sections in the next tasks — leave values blank for now.
verify: `.handoff-secrets.yaml` exists, mode 600, and is not tracked by git.

title: Fill host inventory in secrets vault
steps: ✓ Complete — onboarding is done: the secrets vault, SSH aliases, sudo, and the agent-session flow are all live. This checklist item was folded into the staged Runs 0–7; kept for history.
Open `.handoff-secrets.yaml` and set `_meta.tailnet` to your MagicDNS suffix (Tailscale admin → DNS).
Fill `hosts.nas`, `hosts.mini`, `hosts.rig`, `hosts.seedbox` — LAN IPs match the Network tab (Trusted VLAN 10).
Set `hosts.rig.wol_mac` from your rig NIC (used by `scripts/gaming/wake-rig.sh` — recovery tooling; the rig is 24/7).
Optional: fill `hosts.ha` when HA Green is plugged in (192.168.10.13 reservation).
verify: Every host you plan to hand off has ssh_user, LAN IP or tailnet name, and rig has wol_mac.

title: Gather API keys from services already running
steps: ✓ Complete — onboarding is done: the secrets vault, SSH aliases, sudo, and the agent-session flow are all live. This checklist item was folded into the staged Runs 0–7; kept for history.
From each *arr on the NAS (Sonarr :8989, Radarr :7878, Lidarr :8686, Readarr :8787, Prowlarr :9696): Settings → General → copy API key into `arr_api_keys` in the vault.
Plex: copy claim token or copy `X-Plex-Token` into `plex.token` (NAS at :32400).
Deluge on Betty: Web UI password → `deluge.password` (needed for remote path mappings).
Skip keys for apps not deployed yet — the agent can generate those during deploy.
verify: Vault has non-empty arr_api_keys for every *arr you use today, plus plex.token and deluge.password.

title: Create Backblaze B2 bucket + application key
steps: Backblaze B2 console → Create bucket for NAS Hyper Backup (e.g. `homelab-nas-tier1`).
Create a second bucket or prefix for restic on mini/rig (e.g. `homelab-restic`).
Application Keys → create key scoped to those buckets (read + write).
Paste `account_id` and `application_key` into `backblaze_b2` in the vault.
Generate restic repo passwords: `openssl rand -base64 48` — one for mini, one for rig — store in vault AND your password manager.
verify: Vault has B2 account_id, application_key, bucket names, and two unique restic passwords saved in your password manager too.

title: (Optional) Add cloud creds for upcoming batches
steps: Only fill sections you need for the batch you're about to run — skip the rest.
**Off-site backup batch:** Hetzner Storage Box username (Borg, port 23).
**Ops wave 2 (Caddy HTTPS):** Cloudflare API token with Zone.DNS edit.
**sec-03:** Healthchecks.io ping URLs for restic/Borg dead-man switches.
**Smart home batch:** UniFi Protect local creds, Hue bridge IP, Soulseek/slskd passwords.
**Forgejo (glue-05):** admin username + strong password.
verify: Optional vault sections for your chosen batch are filled; empty sections are fine for later.

title: Choose how sudo works during agent sessions
steps: ✓ Complete — onboarding is done: the secrets vault, SSH aliases, sudo, and the agent-session flow are all live. This checklist item was folded into the staged Runs 0–7; kept for history.
Pick one approach and note it in the vault under each host's `sudo_mode`:
**password** (recommended): store `sudo.mini_password` / `sudo.nas_password` in the vault — agent asks once per session; delete after cleanup.
**present**: you stay at the MacBook and type sudo when prompted — slowest but no stored passwords.
**nopasswd-snippet** (advanced): temporary narrow `/etc/sudoers.d/cursor-handoff` on mini only — remove in handoff-12.
NAS DSM sudo often requires your DSM admin password for `ssh -t nas 'sudo ...'`.
verify: You know which sudo_mode each host uses and the vault reflects it.

title: Physical prep — do only when starting that track
steps: ✓ Complete — onboarding is done: the secrets vault, SSH aliases, sudo, and the agent-session flow are all live. This checklist item was folded into the staged Runs 0–7; kept for history.
Not needed for the first agent batch (ops wave 1). Complete items when you reach the related rollout track:
**Smart home (ha-02):** Plug HA Green into Trusted VLAN; UniFi DHCP reservation 192.168.10.13; enable Advanced SSH add-on.
**Zigbee (ha-12):** Insert USB coordinator into HA Green (use a short USB-2 extension).
**Tier-2 backup (nas-03):** Plug external HDD into NAS USB; note DSM mount path.
**Gaming (game-11):** HDMI dummy plug on rig GPU if no monitor attached.
Check off this task when you've read the list — individual items get checked when done.
verify: You know which physical steps apply to which rollout track; hardware is ready before the agent starts that track.

title: Pick your first agent batch in the rollout guide
steps: ✓ Complete — onboarding is done: the secrets vault, SSH aliases, sudo, and the agent-session flow are all live. This checklist item was folded into the staged Runs 0–7; kept for history.
Click **AI handoff only** in the header filter to see delegatable rollout tasks.
Recommended first batch — **Ops wave 1:** `docker-07` AdGuard → `docker-08` Dockge → `docker-09` ntfy → `docker-10` Beszel → `glue-05` Forgejo (all compose deploys on mini).
Alternative if backbone first: `nas-01` snapshots + `glue-01` UPS/NUT.
Write the task IDs you want in `_meta.notes` in the vault so the agent has a explicit scope.
verify: You have a short ordered list of rollout task IDs for the first agent session.

title: Start a Cursor agent session (share path, not secrets)
steps: ✓ Complete — onboarding is done: the secrets vault, SSH aliases, sudo, and the agent-session flow are all live. This checklist item was folded into the staged Runs 0–7; kept for history.

title: 15-minute verification window after each batch
steps: ✓ Complete — onboarding is done: the secrets vault, SSH aliases, sudo, and the agent-session flow are all live. This checklist item was folded into the staged Runs 0–7; kept for history.
When the agent finishes a deploy batch, block 15 minutes for spot-checks.

**E2E music (seed-10):** request in MusicSeerr; confirm in Plex or Navidrome.

Reply with one-word confirmations in chat; no need to debug logs yourself unless something fails.

title: Post-sprint cleanup — delete vault and rotate keys
steps: Delete `foss-setup/.handoff-secrets.yaml` (or shred: `rm -P .handoff-secrets.yaml`).
Revoke or rotate B2 application keys created only for the handoff sprint; issue new scoped keys if backups keep running.
Remove any temporary `/etc/sudoers.d/cursor-handoff` on mini.
Export rollout progress JSON (header → Export progress) before closing the browser tab.

title: Operator station: clone foss-setup and verify SSH/scp
steps: On your MacBook: ensure Xcode Command Line Tools are installed (`xcode-select --install` if `git` is missing).
Clone or copy this repo to `~/Documents/Home/foss-setup` (USB/offline copy is fine until Forgejo exists in the ops track).
Confirm SSH client tools work: `ssh -V` and `scp` are available.
All later tasks assume you run `scp`/`ssh` from this MacBook once Tailscale and SSH keys land in net-14.
commands: test -d ~/Documents/Home/foss-setup && echo ok || echo 'clone repo first'
verify: The foss-setup repo exists at ~/Documents/Home/foss-setup and ssh/scp run without error.

title: Desktop baseline on CachyOS: browser(s) + LibreOffice (anytime)
steps: On your MacBook: open this task's verify block and keep the repo at ~/Documents/Home/foss-setup handy.
`scp ~/Documents/Home/foss-setup/scripts/setup/cachyos-desktop-baseline.sh rig:/tmp/`
`ssh rig 'bash /tmp/cachyos-desktop-baseline.sh'` — browsers + LibreOffice
Set default browser; pin favorites
Reboot optional; verify desktop apps launch
verify: Firefox/Chromium and LibreOffice open on rig.

title: Set Kagi as the default search engine (anytime)
steps: On your MacBook: open this task's verify block and keep the repo at ~/Documents/Home/foss-setup handy.
Prerequisite: glue-02 complete.
On rig: set Kagi as default search in browser settings
Install Kagi browser extension if desired
Document choice in dotfiles if tracked
verify: Address bar searches use Kagi.

title: Version-controlled dotfiles with chezmoi — MacBook bootstrap
steps: On your MacBook: open this task's verify block and keep the repo at ~/Documents/Home/foss-setup handy.
MacBook: `brew install chezmoi` (or `~/Documents/Home/foss-setup/scripts/dotfiles/bootstrap-dotfiles.sh` with `CHEZMOI_NO_APPLY=1`).
`chezmoi init` — import live configs: `chezmoi add ~/.config/fish ~/.config/nvim ~/.config/alacritty ~/.gitconfig` (adjust paths).
Add homelab SSH config: `cp ~/Documents/Home/foss-setup/configs/network/ssh-config.example ~/.ssh/config`, edit tailnet/users, `chezmoi add --encrypt ~/.ssh/config` (age key in Proton Pass).
`chezmoi apply` → `chezmoi cd` → commit and push to GitHub (`git branch -M main` if branch is still `master`).
MacBook verify: `chezmoi diff` empty; `chezmoi managed` lists fish/nvim/alacritty/ssh/gitconfig.
Continue to **glue-04b** to bootstrap the CachyOS rig and Mac mini.
commands: chezmoi cd && git branch -M main && git push -u origin main
verify: GitHub `btabaska/dotfiles` has chezmoi source layout; MacBook `chezmoi diff` is empty.

title: Apply chezmoi dotfiles to rig + mini (fleet rollout)
steps: On your MacBook: open this task's verify block and keep the repo at ~/Documents/Home/foss-setup handy.
Prerequisite: glue-04 complete.
**Before bootstrapping other hosts:** confirm the age private key (`~/.config/chezmoi/key.txt`) is backed up in Proton Pass — encrypted SSH config won't decrypt without it.

**CachyOS rig** (fish/nvim/alacritty + ssh): `scp ~/Documents/Home/foss-setup/scripts/dotfiles/bootstrap-dotfiles.sh rig:/tmp/` then `ssh rig 'DOTFILES_REPO=btabaska bash /tmp/bootstrap-dotfiles.sh'`.
**Mac mini** (ssh + gitconfig): `scp ~/Documents/Home/foss-setup/scripts/dotfiles/bootstrap-dotfiles.sh mini:/tmp/` then `ssh mini 'DOTFILES_REPO=btabaska bash /tmp/bootstrap-dotfiles.sh'`.
On each host after bootstrap: copy age key from Proton Pass to `~/.config/chezmoi/key.txt` if SSH config is encrypted, then `chezmoi apply`.
Verify fleet-wide from MacBook: `ssh rig 'chezmoi diff && chezmoi status'` and `ssh mini 'chezmoi diff && chezmoi status'` — both diffs must be empty.
Test SSH aliases: `ssh mini hostname`, `ssh rig hostname`.
**Ongoing sync:** edit on one box → `chezmoi edit` → `chezmoi apply` → `chezmoi cd && git push`; on others → `chezmoi update`. Use `.tmpl` files for per-host differences (see chezmoi-quickstart.md).
glue-08 ansible-pull will automate `chezmoi init --apply` later; manual bootstrap is fine until Forgejo (glue-05) is up.
From MacBook: confirm completion matches the verify block before checking this task done.
verify: `chezmoi diff` empty on rig and mini; `ssh mini` / `ssh rig` work from MacBook.

title: Back up your UniFi config (the Zone-Based Firewall is a one-way door)
steps: Open the UniFi Network app on the Dream Wall (unifi.ui.com or local IP).
Go to Settings → System → Backups.
Click Download, choose Settings Only, and save the .unf file off-device (NAS + a second location).
Confirm UniFi Network is on 9.x. Enabling ZBF auto-migrates existing rules and cannot be reverted.
verify: A Settings-Only .unf backup exists in two locations and UniFi Network reports version 9.x.

title: Create the VLAN networks
steps: Settings → Networks → New Virtual Network for each: Trusted (VLAN 10), IoT (VLAN 20), Cameras (VLAN 30, optional), Work (VLAN 40).
For Guest (VLAN 50), use the Guest/Hotspot network type for built-in client isolation.
Set host/gateway per vlan-zone-firewall-plan.md (e.g. Trusted 192.168.10.1/24, IoT 192.168.20.1/24). Keep DHCP on.
Leave Default (VLAN 1) as management — UniFi gear only, no clients.
**Trusted VLAN hosts:** PCs, NAS (.10), Mac mini (.11), rig (.12), **HA Green (.13)**, phones, consoles, Apollo + Moonlight clients.
Do NOT create a gaming VLAN — Moonlight needs same-subnet mDNS on Trusted.
verify: Each network appears in Settings → Networks with its VLAN ID/subnet and is handing out DHCP leases.

title: Create matching Wi-Fi SSIDs and assign switch ports
steps: Settings → WiFi → create only the SSIDs that need wireless (e.g. Home-IoT → IoT, Guest → Guest). Extra SSIDs eat airtime.
Keep Trusted devices (PCs, NAS, phones, consoles, Apollo host + Moonlight clients) on the Trusted SSID/ports.
For wired devices, set the switch port's Native VLAN / Port Profile to the correct network.
Do NOT create a separate gaming VLAN — Moonlight needs same-subnet mDNS.
verify: A test client on each SSID/port gets an IP in the correct subnet; gaming devices land on Trusted.

title: Enable the Zone-Based Firewall and review the migration
steps: Settings → Security → Zone-Based Firewall → enable/upgrade (needs Gateway v4.1+ and Network 9.0.108+).
Confirm the net-00 backup exists — this cannot be undone.
After migration, review the auto-generated policies and delete redundant ones.
verify: Settings shows the Policy Engine zone matrix; internet still works from a Trusted client.

title: Define zones and assign each VLAN
steps: In the ZBF Zones tab, create custom zones: Trusted, IoT, Cameras, Work (Guest uses Hotspot; mgmt uses Internal).
Assign exactly one zone per network per the mapping in vlan-zone-firewall-plan.md.
Leave the six predefined zones (Internal, External, Gateway, VPN, Hotspot, DMZ) in place — DMZ is unused here (no public-facing servers).
verify: Each VLAN shows its assigned zone in the Zones tab.

title: Create firewall policies in priority order
steps: Phase A: confirm each internal zone has → Gateway: Allow (DHCP/DNS/NTP) — policies 5–9 — or clients lose DNS/IP.
Phase B: add specific allows ABOVE broad blocks (policies 1–4, 10–13): Trusted→IoT, Trusted→Cameras, internet allows.
Phase C: add isolation blocks below allows (policies 14–19): IoT→Trusted, Cameras→everything, Work/Guest→all internal.
Phase D: reorder to match firewall-policy-order.md, delete ZBF migration duplicates.
Keep Auto Allow Return Traffic on (stateful); add a description to each policy.
Use firewall-policy-checklist.md — tick-box guide with exact UniFi field values.
verify: Trusted reaches IoT; IoT cannot ping Trusted; Cameras have no internet; Work/Guest reach only the internet.

title: Configure mDNS proxy and IGMP snooping (keep casting + smart home working)
steps: Enable the Gateway mDNS Proxy on BOTH the source and destination VLANs that need cross-VLAN discovery (e.g. Trusted + IoT).

If using Custom service strings (not Auto), include the ones your devices use — AirPlay/Cast/HomeKit/printers AND _esphome._tcp.local so HA on Trusted discovers ESPHome devices (e.g. the Midea SLWF-01 dongle) on IoT.
Turn IGMP Snooping OFF — UniFi's is aggressive and drops Apple TV / HomePod / Matter discovery packets.
No mDNS proxy needed for game streaming — Apollo/Moonlight stay on the same Trusted subnet.
Use mdns-multicast-checklist.md.
verify: A Trusted phone discovers AND controls an IoT cast/AirPlay device; Moonlight auto-discovers the Apollo host on Trusted.

title: Install Tailscale on Ubuntu Server (Mac mini)
steps: Run the install+up script; it adds the upstream apt repo, installs, enables tailscaled, and brings the node up.
Authenticate via the printed URL (or set TS_AUTHKEY for unattended).
Note the node's 100.x Tailscale IP for later tests.
verify: tailscale status lists the node as active and tailscale ip -4 returns a 100.x address.

title: Install Tailscale on the NAS and phone
steps: DS920+: DSM Package Center → search Tailscale → Install, open the app and log in to the tailnet.
For a newer build, manually install the matching x86_64 DSM7 .spk from the Tailscale package server.
Phone: install the Tailscale app and sign in to the same tailnet.
If using the Synology firewall, allow the Tailscale CGNAT range 100.64.0.0/10.
verify: NAS and phone both appear as online machines in the Tailscale admin console.

title: Install Tailscale on the CachyOS rig and your laptop
steps: CachyOS rig: reuse the install script (it auto-detects pacman) or run 'sudo pacman -S tailscale && sudo systemctl enable --now tailscaled && sudo tailscale up'.
Laptop: install Tailscale for its OS (macOS/Windows/Linux) from tailscale.com/download and sign in to the same tailnet.
This completes the Phase-1 rollout's 'Tailscale on NAS, Mac mini, rig, laptop, and phone'.
The rig is 24/7 (decision 2026-07-08) — Tailscale is always up; Wake-on-LAN is kept only as recovery tooling.
verify: The rig and laptop both appear as online machines in the Tailscale admin console.

title: Verify peers are direct (not DERP-relayed)
steps: Run the verify script; it runs netcheck + per-peer status, flagging relayed peers, symmetric NAT, or blocked UDP.
If stuck on DERP: enable UPnP/PCP on the Dream Wall or forward UDP/41641; allow outbound UDP/41641 + UDP/3478.
On Ubuntu with ufw: sudo ufw allow 41641/udp.
Re-run until peers show direct.
verify: tailscale status shows direct for each peer and tailscale netcheck reports UDP: true.

title: Run an end-to-end connectivity health check
steps: Run the connectivity test to ping every peer over the tailnet and TCP-probe key service ports. In Phase 1 only the NAS (5001) and SSH (22) exist, so probe those.
Once Home Assistant is up (Phase 2), re-run adding 8123: PORTS="22 5001 8123".
Re-run anytime as a routine health check — it is read-only and idempotent.
verify: Script exits 0 — all enrolled peers reachable and the probed ports (22, 5001) open.

title: UniFi security extras: IDS/IPS, WPA3, per-SSID mapping
steps: Settings → Security → Threat Management: enable IDS/IPS on the Dream Wall.
Use WPA3 (or WPA2/WPA3 transition) where devices support it; map one SSID each to Trusted, IoT, Guest, and Work.
Network-wide DNS filtering lands in **docker-07 (AdGuard Home — chosen)** + **dns-01 (Unbound recursive)**; point each VLAN's DHCP DNS there once up.
Once Hue is fully local in HA, block the Hue bridge from the internet on the IoT VLAN.
verify: Threat Management shows active and logs events; each SSID lands clients on its mapped VLAN with WPA3 where supported.

title: Enable Tailscale SSH for key-less, ACL-gated admin access
steps: Re-run 'tailscale up --ssh' (or 'tailscale set --ssh') on every node — Mac mini, NAS, rig, and the seedbox once it joins — so each accepts SSH over the tailnet: no authorized_keys to distribute, no port 22 exposed to the internet, and it works the same in-home and remote.

In the Tailscale admin console, tag your admin devices (laptop, phone) tag:admin and your hosts tag:server, then apply the ssh policy from tailscale-acl-ssh.hujson so only tag:admin may SSH tag:server as the chosen Unix user.
Set a checkPeriod (e.g. 12h) so a lost admin device must re-authenticate; de-authing a node in the console instantly revokes its access fleet-wide (far better than rotating keys on five hosts).
Optional: enable session recording to a recorder node for an audit trail of maintenance sessions.
After this, 'ssh <user>@<tailnet-name>' just works from any tag:admin device with no key prompt beyond the tailnet identity.
commands: ssh you@macmini   # over the tailnet, no key needed
verify: From a tag:admin device, 'ssh <user>@<host-tailnet-name>' connects with no key prompt and 'tailscale status' lists the peer; a non-admin device is refused by the ACL.

title: SSH key + ~/.ssh/config fallback across all hosts (break-glass)
steps: On your MacBook: open this task's verify block and keep the repo at ~/Documents/Home/foss-setup handy.
Prerequisite: net-13 complete.
MacBook: `ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C homelab-maint -f ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_homelab`
Per host: DSM → Control Panel → Terminal & SNMP → Enable SSH; Ubuntu: `sudo systemctl enable --now ssh`
`ssh-copy-id -i ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_homelab.pub user@192.168.10.4` (nas), `.11` (mini), and `.12` (rig — 24/7)
`cp ~/Documents/Home/foss-setup/configs/network/ssh-config.example ~/.ssh/config` — set tailnet names/users; `chmod 600 ~/.ssh/config`
Test: `ssh nas hostname`, `ssh mini hostname`, `ssh rig hostname`
Document break-glass path in configs/network/ssh-maintenance-access.md
verify: MacBook `ssh nas` and `ssh mini` work without password; ~/.ssh/config IdentityFile matches id_ed25519_homelab.

hardware: 2× external USB HDDs for Tier 2 off-site rotation; optionally a 4th NAS drive (bay 4) if a single volume outgrows its disk
title: Pre-flight: audit three Basic volumes and map library paths
steps: Prerequisite: net-08 complete.
MacBook: `ssh nas` → record used space: `df -h /volume1 /volume2 /volume3`
Open Storage Manager in DSM; confirm **three separate Basic pools** (~14.6 / 10.9 / 16.4 TB usable) — not SHR, not merged.
Map where TV, movies, music, books, and Tier 1 data live **today** on each volume (not just vol1) — see nas-storage-schema.md §5 runbook.
Confirm Tailscale on NAS (net-08) before any bulk data moves.
Optional: `ssh -t nas 'sudo btrfs filesystem usage /volume1 /volume2 /volume3'` for detailed audit

docs.title: Which RAID type should I choose?

verify: Written path map shows target placement: TV→vol3/tv, movies→vol2/movies, music/books/Tier1→vol1; notes which libraries need moving vs already correct.

title: Prepare vol2 & vol3 Basic pools (wipe only if unorganized)
steps: Prerequisite: nas-00a complete.
**Only if vol2/vol3 are unorganized:** DSM → Control Panel → Shared Folder → delete misplaced media shares on vol2/vol3.
**Only if needed:** Storage Manager → wipe vol2/vol3; recreate empty Basic Btrfs volumes on bays 2 and 3. Do **not** convert to SHR or merge pools.
If vol2/vol3 already hold movies/TV in the right place, **skip wipe** — go straight to nas-00c.
Do NOT touch vol1 — docker, Tier 1, and rsync sources stay until nas-00c completes.
Storage Manager → confirm three **independent** Basic pools (~14.6 / 10.9 / 16.4 TB usable).

docs.title: Which RAID type should I choose?

verify: Three separate Basic pools healthy; vol2 ready for movies, vol3 ready for TV (empty or already correct). Vol1 untouched.

title: Create DSM shares on each Basic volume (+ Btrfs settings)
steps: Prerequisite: nas-00b complete. Safety: net-08 (Tailscale on NAS) before bulk data moves.
You have **three independent Basic volumes** — each share lives on **one volume only** (vol3=TV, vol2=movies, vol1=music/books/Tier1/docker). No merged pool.
**Volume 3:** create share **`tv`** on Volume 3. **Volume 2:** create **`movies`** on Volume 2.
**Volume 1:** create shares: music, books, youtube, games, manual, photo, docs, appdata, backups, vault, home, staging, frigate, cache — all on Volume 1.
Infrastructure dirs (not shares): `ssh -t nas 'sudo mkdir -p /volume1/docker /volume1/mounts/seedbox-files /volume1/scripts/media'`
Btrfs: checksums ON; compression ON for docs/appdata/vault on vol1; OFF for media; atime OFF.
Move data only where nas-00a map shows wrong placement: TV→/volume3/tv, movies→/volume2/movies; music/books into vol1 shares if still flat on vol1.
Verify rsync: `du -sb` byte counts match; dry-run `rsync -avhn --delete` shows zero pending.
Keep vol1 duplicates until nas-00d (NFS/SMB exports) works and **nas-10 + nas-21** confirm Plex/*arr see the new paths.
verify: Each volume holds only its role: vol3/tv, vol2/movies, vol1/music+books+Tier1+docker infra. Byte counts match after any rsync.

title: Enable Container Manager on the NAS (Docker/Compose prerequisite)
steps: On your MacBook browser: sign in to DSM at the NAS LAN or Tailscale address.
Package Center → search **Container Manager** → Install (DSM 7.2+).
Open Container Manager once to accept terms and confirm the daemon starts.
For CLI deploys from net-14: Control Panel → Terminal & SNMP → Enable SSH service (if not already).
Verify: Container Manager shows Container / Project tabs with no errors.
commands: ssh nas 'docker compose version 2>/dev/null || /usr/local/bin/docker compose version'
verify: Container Manager is installed and `docker version` succeeds on the NAS (via DSM or ssh).

title: Network-drive schema: SMB/NFS exports, groups, and permissions
steps: On your MacBook: open this task's verify block and keep the repo at ~/Documents/Home/foss-setup handy.
Prerequisite: nas-00c, net-01 complete.
DSM → Control Panel → Group: create household, media, docker-service groups
DSM → Control Panel → Shared Folder → Edit each share: NFS/SMB permissions per schema
Control Panel → File Services → SMB: min SMB2, max SMB3; enable Bonjour
Control Panel → File Services → NFS: export tv,movies,music to Trusted 192.168.10.0/24 only, squash
MacBook test mount: `mkdir -p ~/mnt/test && mount -t nfs nas:/volume3/tv ~/mnt/test` (from LAN or Tailscale subnet route)
verify: Trusted client mounts NFS exports; SMB works for household.

title: NAS music mount at /mnt/nas/music (music share reachable from consumer hosts)
steps: RETROACTIVE DEFINITION (fix-44, 2026-07-19, audit finding M46): this task was closed as verified live-done in the 2026-07-13 roadmap prune, but its definition was never added to tasks.json, leaving an orphan done id. Recorded here so every progress.json status id resolves; the work itself was already complete and verified.

`ssh -t nas 'grep -q user_allow_other /etc/fuse.conf || echo user_allow_other | sudo tee -a /etc/fuse.conf'`
`ssh -t nas 'sudo test -x /usr/bin/fusermount && sudo ln -sf /usr/bin/fusermount /usr/local/bin/fusermount3'`

verify: Mount lists Betty files/ tree; watchdog scheduled.
summary: Create a **persistent, self-healing rclone SFTP mount** on the NAS that exposes Betty’s `~/files` tree at `/volume1/mounts/seedbox-files`. The *arr containers bind this at `/seedbox:rslave` and import finished downloads from it. A dropped FUSE mount silently stalls **all** imports, so a boot task mounts it and a 5-minute watchdog remounts if it goes stale.

sub: **betty-01 complete** — labels + `~/files` tree exist on Betty.
SSH alias `nas` works; you can `sudo` on the NAS. rclone installed on DSM (SynoCommunity package or the static binary — confirm with `which rclone`).

detail.h: Step 1 — Copy the config + scripts to the NAS
Step 2 — Generate a root SSH key and authorize it on Betty
body: Task Scheduler runs these scripts as **root**, so the key and `known_hosts` must live under `/root/.ssh` (absolute paths — a `~` would expand to whoever runs rclone).
detail.note: Pinning the host key in `/root/.ssh/known_hosts` defeats MITM and stops the first-run prompt from blocking the unattended mount.

detail.h: Step 3 — Install the rclone remote definition
body: Copy the template into place and lock its permissions. Edit it only if your rclone path differs:
sub: The `[seedbox]` remote uses key-based SFTP auth (`key_file = /root/.ssh/seedbox_ed25519`). The `lsd` test should list `tv movies music books manual`.

detail.h: Step 4 — Install the scripts + enable allow_other
body: `--allow-other` lets the *arr containers’ PUID/PGID read the mount; it requires `user_allow_other` in `/etc/fuse.conf`:
cmds: ssh -t nas 'grep -q user_allow_other /etc/fuse.conf || echo user_allow_other | sudo tee -a /etc/fuse.conf'

sub: You should see the `tv movies music books manual` tree. The mount uses `--vfs-cache-mode writes --dir-cache-time 1m --buffer-size 64M` (safe writes, fast pickup of new downloads, smooth import copies).

detail.h: Step 6 — Schedule boot mount + 5-min watchdog (DSM Task Scheduler)
body: Synology has no systemd. In **Control Panel → Task Scheduler**, create two **user-defined script** tasks, both **User = root**:
detail.note: The watchdog treats the mount as unhealthy if it isn’t a mountpoint **or** `ls` fails/hangs (stale SFTP handle), then tears it down and remounts. Without it, a dead mount makes `/seedbox` look empty and every import silently does nothing.

pitfalls: Generating the key as your user instead of root — Task Scheduler runs as root and won’t find a key under your home dir.
Skipping `user_allow_other` in `/etc/fuse.conf` — `--allow-other` fails and the containers can’t read the mount.
Forgetting the watchdog — a single dropped mount stalls all four *arrs with no error.
Wrong rclone path in the scripts — set `RCLONE=$(which rclone)` if it isn’t `/usr/local/bin/rclone`.

steps: Prerequisite: nas-20 rclone mount live at /volume1/mounts/seedbox-files and nas-prep-01 complete.

cd /volume1/docker/media-automation && cp .env.example .env. Run `id <user>` on DSM — set PUID, PGID, TZ=America/New_York, RG_DB_PASSWORD (openssl rand -hex 24), and path vars for the **three-volume layout**: TV_LIBRARY=/volume3/tv, MOVIES_LIBRARY=/volume2/movies, MUSIC_LIBRARY=/volume1/music, DOCKER_ROOT=/volume1/docker, SEEDBOX_MOUNT=/volume1/mounts/seedbox-files, CWA_INGEST=/volume1/docker/calibre-web-automated/ingest.
**Phase A** (core TV/movies): docker compose up -d prowlarr flaresolverr sonarr radarr
**Phase B** (music + books): docker compose up -d lidarr readarr rreading-glasses rreading-glasses-db
**Phase C** (archives): docker compose up -d unpackerr — only after API keys in unpackerr.conf (nas-25).
All images pinned in docker-compose.yml (never :latest). See README.md RAM-phased checklist.

verify: Book lands in CWA ingest and CWA processes it into /volume1/books.
summary: Stand up the **books lane: Readarr + self-hosted rreading-glasses → Calibre-Web-Automated**. Readarr (EOL, pinned to a final immutable build) uses the local rreading-glasses metadata provider instead of the dead Goodreads service, grabs via Deluge label `readarr`, and drops imports into the **CWA ingest** folder, which converts/organizes into `/volume1/books`.

sub: **nas-22 complete** and **nas-09 complete** (Calibre-Web-Automated deployed; its ingest folder exists).
rreading-glasses + its postgres are up from nas-21 Phase B (`http://rreading-glasses:8788`).

detail.h: Step 1 — Point Readarr at the self-hosted metadata provider
body: The original Readarr metadata service is dead; you must use rreading-glasses. The metadata page is hidden — browse to it directly:
detail.note: rreading-glasses’ Goodreads upstream is backward-compatible with existing Readarr DBs. Don’t point Readarr at a public instance.

body: **Settings → Media Management → Root Folders →** `/cwa-book-ingest` (host `${CWA_INGEST}` = `/volume1/docker/calibre-web-automated/ingest`). Readarr does **not** own the final library — CWA ingests, converts, and organizes into `/volume1/books` for Plex/Kobo.

Confirm the remote Deluge client with label **`readarr`** and the same Remote Path Mapping `/home/hd34/btabaska/files/` → `/seedbox/`. Remove Completed OFF. Add a book-capable indexer in Prowlarr and Full-Sync.

cmds: ssh nas 'docker compose -f /volume1/docker/media-automation/docker-compose.yml ps readarr rreading-glasses'
sub: Grab a public-domain title; confirm the file lands in the CWA ingest folder and CWA processes it into `/volume1/books`.

pitfalls: Leaving Readarr’s metadata provider on the default (dead) service — searches/metadata fail. It must be `http://rreading-glasses:8788`.
Setting Readarr’s root to the final books library instead of the **ingest** folder — CWA never gets to convert/organize.
Using the floating `develop` tag — the compose pins the final immutable build on purpose.
rreading-glasses eating RAM — the compose caps it at `mem_limit: 128m` per upstream guidance; keep the cap.

title: Unpackerr — extract archived releases for the *arrs
steps: Prerequisite: nas-22 complete.
Edit /volume1/docker/media-automation/unpackerr/unpackerr.conf — fill Sonarr/Radarr/Lidarr/Readarr API keys.
`docker compose up -d unpackerr` on NAS.
Test: Deluge label with RAR release → Unpackerr extracts → *arr imports.
commands: ssh nas 'cd /volume1/docker/media-automation && docker compose up -d unpackerr'
verify: RAR release auto-imported.
summary: Deploy **Unpackerr** (Phase C) to auto-extract multi-part RAR/ZIP releases so the *arrs can import them. It watches each *arr’s queue over the API and extracts in place on the `/seedbox` mount — **never deleting originals** (they’re still seeding on Betty), only the extracted files after import.

sub: **nas-22 complete.** You have each *arr’s API key (Settings → General).

body: Edit `unpackerr/unpackerr.conf` and replace each `TODO_*_API_KEY`. The `url` values are the in-compose DNS names (`http://sonarr:8989`, etc.) — leave them:
detail.note: Critical safety: every block has `delete_orig = false` (originals keep seeding) and `delete_delay = "10m"` (remove only the **extracted** copies 10 min after import). Do not flip `delete_orig`.

body: Unpackerr acts on the path each *arr **reports** (already Remote-Path-Mapped to `/seedbox/...`). It mounts `/seedbox:rslave` at the **same** in-container path as the *arrs with **no mapping of its own**. The `paths` in the conf (`/seedbox/tv`, `/seedbox/movies`, …) must match.

cmds: ssh nas 'cd /volume1/docker/media-automation && docker compose up -d unpackerr'
sub: Grab a known multi-part RAR release; watch the log extract it and the *arr import the result.

pitfalls: Setting `delete_orig = true` — deletes the seeding source on Betty and breaks ratio.
Giving Unpackerr its own path mapping — it must see `/seedbox` at the identical path as the *arrs (rslave), no mapping.
Wrong API keys — Unpackerr can’t read the queue and silently extracts nothing.

title: Manual lane — the ONE scheduled rclone copy job
steps: Prerequisite: nas-20 rclone mount live.
Copy scripts/media/rclone-manual-copy.sh to /volume1/scripts/media/ on the NAS if not already there.
DSM Task Scheduler: run rclone-manual-copy.sh every 15 minutes as root.
This is the **only** scheduled rclone job — copies Deluge label `manual` → /volume1/manual only.
*arr media (tv/movies/music/books) arrives via the live /seedbox mount + *arr import — never via scheduled copy.
verify: A file in Betty files/manual/ copies to /volume1/manual; no scheduled job touches *arr label folders.
summary: Schedule the **single** rclone job in the whole system — the **manual lane**. Deluge label `manual` → `~/files/manual` on Betty; this job copies that subtree down to `/volume1/manual` every 15 min. It must **never** touch the *arr label folders (those import via the live mount, not a scheduled copy).

sub: **nas-20 complete** (rclone remote + scripts on the NAS).

detail.h: Step 2 — Schedule every 15 min as root (DSM Task Scheduler)
body: Control Panel → Task Scheduler → Create → **Scheduled Task → User-defined script**, User = root, repeat every 15 minutes:
detail.note: It uses `rclone copy` (not move/sync) scoped to `…/files/manual` → `/volume1/manual`, with `--min-age 5m` (skip in-progress files). `copy` keeps the file seeding and is idempotent. Only swap to `rclone move` for throwaway grabs you deliberately don’t want to seed.

pitfalls: Pointing the job at `…/files/` (whole tree) instead of `…/files/manual` — it would double-import *arr media. Keep it scoped to `manual/`.
Using `rclone sync` — sync deletes on the destination to mirror the source; use `copy`.
Adding more scheduled rclone jobs — by design there is exactly **one** (this manual lane). *arr media uses the live mount + import.

title: Media-automation self-check (report violations, don't silently fix)
steps: Prerequisite: nas-22 through nas-26 complete.
Run self-check from configs/nas/media-automation/README.md (docker inspect mounts section).
Confirm: /seedbox on sonarr/radarr/lidarr/readarr/unpackerr; per-volume roots; one scheduled rclone job (manual only).
Soulseek stack (seed-09, nas-29, nas-30) is verified separately — not a blocker for this core self-check.
Report violations — do not silently fix drift.
commands: ssh nas "docker inspect sonarr radarr lidarr readarr unpackerr --format '{{.Name}}{{range .Mounts}} {{.Destination}}({{.Propagation}}){{end}}'"
verify: Core *arr self-check items pass.
summary: Run the media-automation **self-check** — verify the shared invariants hold (identical `/seedbox:rslave` mounts, per-volume library mounts, exactly one scheduled rclone job, Lidarr-only music, Readarr→rreading-glasses). **Report** violations; don’t silently ‘fix’ them.

sub: **nas-22, nas-23, nas-24, nas-25, nas-26 complete.**

body: Every download-touching service must show `/seedbox(rslave)` and only its own library volume:
cmds: ssh nas "docker inspect sonarr radarr lidarr readarr unpackerr --format '{{.Name}}{{range .Mounts}} {{.Destination}}({{.Propagation}}){{end}}'"

detail.h: Step 2 — Confirm exactly one scheduled rclone job
body: In DSM Task Scheduler there should be: boot mount, 5-min watchdog, and **one** 15-min manual-copy. No other scheduled rclone transfers.

sub: Lidarr: root `/music`, rename ON, **no beets/slskd/soularr**.
Readarr: metadata `http://rreading-glasses:8788`, root `/cwa-book-ingest`.
PUID/PGID/TZ identical across services; `/config` under `/volume1/docker/<app>`.

body: Write any violation into `configs/nas/media-automation/README.md` notes (or your runbook). Do not auto-remediate — a surprising mount change usually means something upstream drifted.

pitfalls: ‘Fixing’ a drift in place without recording it — you lose the signal that an invariant broke.
A service missing `(rslave)` — a later remount won’t propagate and imports stall.
A stray second scheduled rclone job — risks double-imports.

title: Deploy Plex Media Server on the NAS (libraries + Quick Sync)

Settings → Transcoder → enable **Use hardware acceleration when available** (Quick Sync).
Verify Home users + resume playback. Add Plex appdata to Hyper Backup Tier 1.
verify: Plex plays with HW transcode; Home users and watch history preserved (or scan-only fallback in README §4).

Prerequisite: seed-07 complete.
Backup UniFi
Remove NAS qbittorrent/gluetun

Remove the NAS qBittorrent/Gluetun containers, undo the dual-LAN routing and 5 Mbps cap, stop any leftover Ubuntu Plex/*arr, and record the cutover date. detail.h: Step 2 — Remove the NAS torrent containers

detail.h: Step 3 — Undo dual-LAN routing + the 5 Mbps cap
body: Remove the policy-based routing rules / second LAN that forced torrent traffic through the VPN, and delete the 5 Mbps shaping rule. Confirm no leftover `ip rule` entries:

cmds: ssh mini 'docker ps -a | grep -iE "plex|sonarr|radarr|lidarr|readarr" || echo moved to NAS'

detail.h: Step 6 — Final check: no P2P/VPN on the NAS
cmds: ssh nas 'ss -tlnp | grep -iE ":51820|wireguard|gluetun" || echo no vpn sockets'

Forgetting the UniFi backup — undoing dual-LAN by hand without a fallback is risky.
Leaving Gluetun/qBittorrent stopped-but-present — remove them so no VPN/P2P sockets linger on the NAS.

title: beets YouTube-audio tagging layer on NAS (tags non-Lidarr music)
steps: NOT redundant with Lidarr after all: Lidarr (writeAudioTags=sync) tags everything IT imports, and those tags flow to the rig ALAC mirror via ffmpeg -map_metadata 0. But MeTube-ingested YouTube audio bypasses Lidarr and arrives with junk/filename-only tags.

beets (media-automation compose, profile music-tags) tags the YouTube path IN PLACE: config beets/config.yaml.youtube-ingest — directory /music, library /config/beets-youtube.db, plugins fromfilename + web, import copy:no move:no write:yes, strong-match-only (weak matches quarantined-by-skip in /music/YouTube).

The always-on container runs `beet web` (the LSIO image's service) — REQUIRES the `web` plugin + web.host 0.0.0.0 or it crash-loops 'unknown command web'. Fixed 2026-07-14; :8337 serves the library browser.
Daily `beet import` tags new YouTube audio; Lidarr owns the rest of /music (never moved/copied by beets).
commands: ssh nas 'cd /volume1/docker/media-automation && docker compose --profile music-tags run --rm beets beet write'
verify: beets web UI serves on :8337 and the daily YouTube-ingest import runs (guarded by nas-beets + nas-beets-ingest-fresh).

title: Install HAOS in a KVM/libvirt VM (alternative path)
steps: **Skip if HA Green is set up (ha-02).** This is the abandoned alternative — HAOS VM on the Mac mini.
The plan decided **HA Green purchased** to keep the always-on hub off the 8GB Mac mini.
Only use this path if the Green hardware fails and you need a temporary VM on the rig (not the mini).
verify: Skipped — HA Green is the primary path (ha-02).
summary: **Alternative to HA Green:** run **HAOS in a KVM/libvirt VM** on the rig. Uses UEFI (`--boot uefi`, OVMF) without secure boot + VirtIO disk/net per the official guide. Skip this if you took the Green path (ha-02).

sub: **ha-01 complete.** libvirt/virt-install on the rig (`apt install virtinst` / pacman `libvirt virt-install`).

The script downloads the HAOS qcow2, preflights the OVMF firmware (`/usr/share/OVMF/OVMF_CODE_4M.fd` — note the uppercase `4M`), and runs `virt-install --boot uefi` so libvirt auto-resolves firmware + creates the per-VM NVRAM.

detail.h: Step 2 — Bridge the NIC to Trusted + open HA
body: Bridge the VM NIC onto the **Trusted** LAN (not NAT) so HA is a first-class device, then browse to the VM’s IP `:8123` and onboard as in ha-02.

pitfalls: Hand-wiring loader/pflash paths — they break on filename/casing drift (`OVMF_CODE_4m.fd` vs `OVMF_CODE_4M.fd`). Use `--boot uefi` and let libvirt resolve it.
Leaving the VM on NAT — bridge it to Trusted so discovery/mDNS and device reachability work.
Running both Green and the VM — pick one HA instance.

title: Install HACS (community store)
steps: On your MacBook: open this task's verify block and keep the repo at ~/Documents/Home/foss-setup handy.

verify: HACS sidebar present.
summary: Install **HACS** (the community integration/store) so you can add `midea_ac_lan`, Bermuda, and other community pieces used later. Download via the SSH add-on, then add it as an integration and restart.

sub: **ha-02 complete** (HA up; SSH/Terminal add-on installed).

body: Open the **Terminal & SSH** add-on web terminal and run the official download script, then **restart Home Assistant**:
cmds: # Settings → Developer Tools → Restart (or `ha core restart`)

body: After restart: **Settings → Devices & Services → Add Integration → HACS**, accept the prompts, and complete the **GitHub device login**. HACS appears in the sidebar.
Restart HA Core after the first HACS setup so the store fully initializes. Now you can install HACS integrations as needed.

pitfalls: Forgetting to restart after the download script — HACS won’t appear as an integration.
GitHub device-login step skipped — HACS can’t fetch repositories without it.
Installing community integrations you don’t vet — HACS code runs in your HA; stick to well-known repos.

title: Add Philips Hue (local LAN via Hue Bridge)
steps: On your MacBook: open this task's verify block and keep the repo at ~/Documents/Home/foss-setup handy.
Prerequisite: ha-02 complete.
Settings → Devices → Add Integration → Philips Hue
Press Hue bridge button when prompted
Assign Hue entities to areas
Move Hue bridge to IoT VLAN per net-02
verify: Hue lights controllable locally.
summary: Add **Philips Hue** locally via the Hue Bridge (no cloud at runtime). Pair by pressing the bridge button, assign lights to areas, then move the bridge onto the **IoT VLAN** per net-02.

sub: **ha-02 complete.**

body: **Settings → Devices & Services → Add Integration → Philips Hue**. When prompted, **press the link button** on the Hue Bridge to authorize. Assign the discovered lights/rooms to HA **areas**.

detail.h: Step 2 — Move the bridge to IoT
body: Relocate the Hue Bridge onto the **IoT VLAN** (net-02). HA (Trusted) reaches it via the firewall policy; the bridge talks to bulbs over Zigbee regardless.

detail.note: Mostly done already: Hue Bridge integrated (73 lights, 21 scenes), bridge ALREADY on IoT VLAN 20 at 192.168.20.100 (vault: hue.bridge_ip) with 0 B internet traffic — effectively cloud-isolated. Remaining: 16 lights unavailable (wall-switch power cuts — see shopping list), finish area assignment after the human answers the room-mapping questions in https://wiki.tabaska.us/reference/home-assistant/home-context/.

pitfalls: Bridge and HA on different VLANs with discovery blocked — add a firewall allow for HA→bridge, or add by IP.
Relying on the Hue cloud — this integration is local; you don’t need a Hue account online for control.

title: Thermostat: install ecobee Premium (in box) — local HomeKit control; retire Nest SDM plan
steps: DECISION 2026-07-08: the new-in-box **ecobee Smart Thermostat Premium** replaces the installed Nest 3rd gen. The old Google SDM cloud path (this task’s previous scope) is DROPPED — no Google Cloud project, no $5 fee, no cloud dependency.

HUMAN — install at the boiler: kill power to the boiler circuit, photograph existing Nest wiring (likely 2-wire Rh/W from the boiler relay), swap the wall plate. If only 2 wires, use the included Power Extender Kit at the boiler per ecobee’s boiler guide.
HUMAN — ecobee onboarding on the unit itself: join the **IoT SSID**; skip/minimize ecobee account features. Note the HomeKit pairing QR/code on screen.
AI — add via Settings → Devices → Add → Apple HomeKit Device (homekit_controller) using the pairing code: fully LOCAL control + built-in air-quality & occupancy sensors as HA entities.
Decommission the Nest (factory-reset, sell/spare). Delete any half-made Google Device Access artifacts.
Bonus: ecobee pairs natively with Siri already; still expose the HA climate entity via HomeKit Bridge (ha-16) for one consistent Apple Home.
verify: climate.* entity live in HA via homekit_controller; setpoint change from HA moves the physical thermostat; works with WAN blocked for the device.
summary: Wire **Google Nest** via the **Smart Device Management (SDM) API** — the fiddliest integration: a Google Cloud project, a one-time **$5** Device Access registration, an OAuth client, and a Pub/Sub topic for push events. It is **cloud-routed** (not fully local). Budget ~45–60 min, do it in one sitting, follow the repo checklist.

sub: **ha-02 complete.** Use a **personal @gmail.com** account that owns the Nest devices — Workspace and Advanced-Protection accounts CANNOT use SDM.
Nest devices already working in the Google Home app; confirm yours is SDM-supported before paying the $5.
HA reachable over HTTPS, or use the My Home Assistant redirect `https://my.home-assistant.io/redirect/oauth`. Bitwarden open for the IDs.

body: In the Google Cloud Console create/choose a project (e.g. `home-assistant-nest`); note the **Project ID**. Enable both **Smart Device Management API** and **Cloud Pub/Sub API**.

detail.h: Step 2 — OAuth client + Device Access project
body: Configure the OAuth consent screen, create an **OAuth client ID** (Web application) with the redirect URI matching HA (or the My-HA redirect). Then in the **Device Access Console** pay the one-time **$5**, create a project, and link the OAuth client. Record the **Device Access Project ID**.

detail.h: Step 3 — Add the integration in HA + OAuth
body: **Settings → Devices & Services → Add Integration → Google Nest**, paste the OAuth client ID/secret + Device Access project ID, and complete the Google consent flow. Follow `nest-sdm-oauth-checklist.md` for the Pub/Sub topic/subscription details (the flow changed 2025-01-23).
Store the Cloud Project ID, OAuth Client ID/Secret, Device Access Project ID, and Pub/Sub topic name in Bitwarden.

detail.note: User call: heating season is months away — park the ecobee install. No Nest integration either (Google SDM stays dropped; user won’t use the Nest remotely). Revisit at first cold snap (October); everything else in Run 5 proceeds without it.

pitfalls: Using a Workspace or Advanced-Protection Google account — SDM simply won’t work; use a personal gmail.
OAuth redirect URI mismatch — it must match exactly (HA HTTPS URL or the My-HA redirect).
Skipping the Pub/Sub topic — events/state updates won’t push; the flow changed in 2025, follow the current checklist.
Expecting local control — Nest SDM is cloud-routed; commands go Google cloud → device.

title: Add Midea AC + dehumidifier via midea_ac_lan (local) — and BACK UP the .json
steps: Prerequisite: ha-04 (HACS) complete. Follow configs/homeassistant/midea-local-setup.md Path A.
HACS → install **midea_ac_lan** → restart HA.
Static DHCP on IoT VLAN for each Midea unit. Add integration → one-time cloud handshake for V3 token/key.
**Immediately back up** `/config/.storage/midea_ac_lan/*.json` to NAS + Bitwarden — Midea is closing token APIs.
Test local control with Midea cloud blocked on IoT VLAN (after token captured).
verify: Midea AC responds from HA.
summary: Add the **Midea AC + dehumidifier locally** via the HACS `midea_ac_lan` integration (LAN only, no runtime cloud). **Critical:** back up each V3 device’s `.json` token file off-box the moment it’s added — Midea is shutting down the cloud token APIs, and a lost token means permanent loss of local control.

sub: **ha-04 complete** (HACS installed).

body: HACS → install **`midea_ac_lan`** → restart HA. **Settings → Devices & Services → Add Integration → Midea AC LAN**. Add each device by **IP** (on the IoT VLAN); V3 devices need the auto-discovered or manually entered `device_id`, `token`, `key` (port `6444`).

detail.h: Step 2 — BACK UP the per-device .json (do this immediately)
body: The moment each V3 device is added, copy its token file off the HA box (NAS + password manager):
detail.note: Midea is closing the Meiju/SmartHome/NetHome Plus token APIs. Once gone you cannot regenerate a token — your saved `.json` becomes the ONLY credential. Do not delete/unbind a device ‘to start over’. (V2 devices have no token/.json.)

body: Toggle the AC from HA and confirm it responds with the internet/cloud unreachable (true local control). Tuya-based 2021+ units can fall back to LocalTuya.

pitfalls: Not backing up the `.json` immediately — the #1 way people permanently lose Midea local control.
Unbinding/re-adding a V3 device after the cloud API is gone — you can’t get a new token.
Device on a VLAN HA can’t reach on port 6444 — add the firewall allow.

hardware: SLWF-01Pro v2.1 (ESP8266) dongle — ~$15–20 per AC unit
title: (Optional) Replace Midea OEM dongle with ESPHome SLWF-01Pro (cloud-free)
steps: Optional privacy upgrade after ha-07: replace OEM WiFi dongle with **ESPHome SLWF-01Pro** (~$13).
Flash per configs/homeassistant/esphome-midea-slwf01.yaml (or smlight.tech flasher).
Add ESPHome integration in HA; block original Midea cloud dongle from internet.
Fully cloud-free Midea control — recommended if you are hands-on.
verify: AC controlled via ESPHome only.
summary: **(Optional)** Replace the Midea OEM Wi-Fi dongle with an **ESPHome SLWF-01Pro** (ESP8266) for guaranteed cloud-free local control. Flash the provided ESPHome config, adopt on IoT, then disable the cloud dongle.

sub: **ha-07 complete.** One SLWF-01Pro v2.1 (~$15–20) per AC unit.

body: Flash the firmware in `configs/homeassistant/esphome-midea-slwf01.yaml` via the **ESPHome dashboard** add-on or `web.esphome.io`. Enter the FLH bootloader (hold **FLH**, plug USB-C). The Midea protocol uses the ESP8266 hardware UART at **9600 baud** (the config sets `logger: baud_rate: 0` so it doesn’t clash).
detail.note: Define `api_encryption_key` and Wi-Fi creds in ESPHome `secrets.yaml` before flashing.

detail.h: Step 2 — Adopt on IoT + cut over
body: Plug the dongle into the AC’s USB port. **Settings → Devices & Services → ESPHome** → adopt the device (on the IoT VLAN). Confirm climate control works, then **disable/remove the cloud Midea dongle** integration for that unit.

pitfalls: Wrong board/UART — SLWF-01Pro is ESP8266 (`esp01_1m`), UART 9600; leaving the serial logger on conflicts with the AC UART.
Adopting before disabling the OEM dongle — two integrations fighting over one AC.
Forgetting secrets.yaml — flash fails or device can’t join Wi-Fi/API.

title: Set up local voice (Assist + Whisper + Piper); LLM agent via LiteLLM (ha-17)
steps: On your MacBook: open this task's verify block and keep the repo at ~/Documents/Home/foss-setup handy.
Prerequisite: ha-02 complete.
Install Whisper + Piper add-ons for Assist
Settings → Voice assistants → pipeline
Point conversation agent to LiteLLM (ha-17) for complex queries
Test wake word on phone Companion app
verify: Voice command triggers automation.
summary: Set up **local voice** — HA **Assist** with the **Whisper** (STT) and **Piper** (TTS) add-ons — and route complex questions to your LLM via **LiteLLM** (ha-17). Everything stays on-prem; no cloud assistant.

sub: **ha-02 complete.** LiteLLM (ha-17) for the LLM agent step.

body: Settings → Add-ons → install **Whisper** (speech-to-text) and **Piper** (text-to-speech). Start both; they register as Wyoming services automatically.

Settings → **Voice assistants** → create/edit a pipeline: STT = Whisper, TTS = Piper, and **Conversation agent**. Use HA’s built-in intents for device control; for complex queries point the agent at **LiteLLM** `http://192.168.10.2:4000` (ha-17).
Trigger Assist from the Companion app (or a Voice PE / ESP satellite) and confirm a spoken command runs an automation.

pitfalls: Expecting fast STT on weak hardware — Whisper on HA Green is fine for short commands; large models will lag.
Routing all intents to the LLM — keep device control on built-in intents (fast, deterministic); send only open-ended questions to LiteLLM.
LiteLLM not deployed yet — the LLM-agent part depends on ha-17.

title: Configure the Energy dashboard
steps: Prerequisite: ha-05, ha-07 complete.
Settings → Dashboards → Energy → add grid consumption.
Add Emporia Vue circuits (plan: whole-home + key circuits) if integrated.
Add smart-plug monitors from Zigbee devices as they land.
Useful for validating Section 5 power estimates (always-on ~$150–200/yr; rig 24/7 ~130W idle ≈ $23/mo, tuning target <100W).
verify: Energy dashboard shows live usage.
summary: Configure the HA **Energy dashboard** — pull grid/solar and per-device consumption from your Hue/Nest/Midea (and any plug-level monitors) into one view to track the ‘always-on’ power story.

sub: **ha-05, ha-07 complete** (some power-reporting devices present).

body: Settings → Dashboards → **Energy**. Add your **grid consumption** source (a whole-home monitor or smart meter integration if you have one), **solar** if applicable, and **individual devices** (energy-reporting Hue plugs, Midea, etc.).
Energy stats are long-term; the dashboard fills in over hours/days as the recorder accumulates. Confirm live usage starts showing.

pitfalls: Adding entities that report power (W) but not energy (kWh) as grid sources — the Energy dashboard needs cumulative energy sensors (use a Riemann-sum helper to integrate power into energy if needed).
Expecting instant history — it backfills over time.

title: Configure HA backups to NAS and SAVE the encryption key
steps: On your MacBook: open this task's verify block and keep the repo at ~/Documents/Home/foss-setup handy.
Prerequisite: ha-02 complete.
Install Samba Backup or HA backup to NAS share backups/ha/
Schedule nightly 04:00
Copy encryption key to Bitwarden + paper — loss = unrecoverable backups
Test restore on spare VM or second instance
verify: Backup file lands on NAS nightly.
summary: Configure **HA backups to the NAS** and — most importantly — **save the backup encryption key** (the emergency kit). HA backups are encrypted (SecureTar v3 on 2026.4+) and Nabu Casa **cannot** recover the key. Lose it and the backups are permanently unreadable.

sub: **ha-02 complete.** Update HA to 2026.4+ first (stronger SecureTar v3 format).

detail.h: Step 1 — Set up backups + DOWNLOAD the emergency kit
body: Settings → System → **Backups → Set up backups**. During setup, **download the backup emergency kit** (it contains the encryption key) and **save the key in Bitwarden** plus an offline copy (paper / second vault). Store it **outside** HA — a key only on HA is useless if HA dies.

detail.h: Step 2 — Send backups off-box to the NAS
body: Install the **Samba Backup** add-on, or Settings → System → Storage → **Add network storage** (SMB/NFS to the NAS `backups/ha/` share) and select it as a backup location. Schedule **nightly 04:00**, full backup, keep ~7.
Verify a backup file actually lands on the NAS, then test-restore onto a spare VM/second instance so you know the key + archive actually work.

pitfalls: Storing the encryption key only on HA — if HA dies you can’t decrypt the backups. Put it in Bitwarden + offline.
Never testing a restore — an untested backup is a hope, not a backup.
Backing up only locally — push a copy to the NAS so a dead HA Green isn’t a total loss.

hardware: Zigbee coordinator — Sonoff Dongle Plus-E (~$20) or HA Connect ZBT-2 (~$25) — plus Zigbee sensors & mains smart-plug routers (see the Sensors tab)
title: Zigbee backbone: Mosquitto + Zigbee2MQTT + USB coordinator
steps: Prerequisite: ha-04 complete. Install **Mosquitto** broker add-on on HA Green.
Install **Zigbee2MQTT** add-on; plug USB coordinator into HA Green (Sonoff Zigbee 3.0 Plus-E or HA Connect ZBT-2).
Use a short USB-2 extension cable to avoid USB-3 interference.
Pair first sensor near coordinator, then move it; add mains-powered smart plugs as Zigbee routers (5–7 spread around house).
Z2M → MQTT → HA auto-discovery. Avoid Aqara-branded hubs if mixing brands.
verify: Zigbee sensor updates in HA.
summary: Stand up the **Zigbee backbone**: the **Mosquitto** MQTT broker add-on + **Zigbee2MQTT** add-on driving a **USB coordinator** (Sonoff Dongle Plus-E or HA Connect ZBT-2). Z2M publishes to MQTT and HA auto-discovers devices. Add mains-powered smart plugs as routers to extend the mesh.

sub: **ha-04 complete.** A Zigbee coordinator + a few sensors/mains routers (see the Sensors tab).

detail.h: Step 1 — Plug in the coordinator + find its port
body: Insert the USB coordinator into HA Green (a short USB extension reduces 2.4 GHz interference). Note its stable device path:
Settings → Add-ons → **Mosquitto broker** → install + start. Create an HA user for MQTT if prompted; the MQTT integration auto-configures.

Install the **Zigbee2MQTT** add-on. In its config set the `serial.port` to the `/dev/serial/by-id/...` path from step 1 and the matching `adapter` type for your coordinator. Start it; Z2M → MQTT → HA discovery brings devices in automatically.
In the Z2M UI enable **Permit join** (time-limited), put a sensor into pairing, and confirm it appears in Z2M and then as an HA entity. Add mains plugs first — they act as routers.

pitfalls: Using `/dev/ttyUSB0` instead of `/dev/serial/by-id/...` — the ttyUSB number can change on reboot and break Z2M.
Coordinator plugged directly into a USB3 port/hub — 2.4 GHz interference; use a USB2 extension.
Leaving Permit join on permanently — security risk; enable it only while pairing.
Wrong `adapter` for ZBT-2 vs Sonoff-E — set the type that matches your stick.

hardware: (Optional, for room-level presence) mmWave/Zigbee occupancy sensors (e.g. Sonoff SNZB-06P) and/or a few ESP32 BLE-proxy nodes
title: Presence detection: HA Companion app + room-level (mmWave/Bermuda)
steps: On your MacBook: open this task's verify block and keep the repo at ~/Documents/Home/foss-setup handy.
Prerequisite: ha-02 complete.
Install Companion on phones; enable location
Add mmWave or Bermuda for room presence
Create person-based automations
verify: Arrival automation fires.
summary: Add **presence detection** — the HA **Companion app** for person/home-away, plus optional **room-level** presence via mmWave occupancy sensors and/or **Bermuda** BLE trilateration using ESP32 proxy nodes.

sub: **ha-02 complete.** Optional: mmWave sensors (e.g. Sonoff SNZB-06P) and/or a few ESP32 BLE-proxy nodes.

body: Install the **Home Assistant Companion** app on phones, sign in, and enable **location** permissions. Each phone becomes a `device_tracker` feeding the `person` entities.
Place **mmWave** occupancy sensors for reliable per-room presence, and/or install **Bermuda** (HACS) with a few **ESP32 BLE proxies** to trilaterate phones/tags between rooms.
Create person-based automations (arrival lights, away mode). Test by leaving/returning.

detail.note: Brandon’s iPhone companion app already connected (mobile_app "Btiphone"). Remaining: Kaelyn’s phone + room-level presence (Bermuda via ESP32 BLE proxies — see shopping list in https://wiki.tabaska.us/reference/home-assistant/home-context/).

pitfalls: Relying on Wi-Fi/router presence alone — laggy and flappy; the Companion app GPS + BLE is far better.
mmWave placement — tune sensitivity/zones or it triggers on fans/pets.
Bermuda without enough ESP32 proxies — needs multiple nodes per area to trilaterate.

title: Bring UniFi Protect cameras into Home Assistant (local, no subscription)
steps: On your MacBook: open this task's verify block and keep the repo at ~/Documents/Home/foss-setup handy.
Prerequisite: ha-02 complete.
HACS/integration: UniFi Protect
Enter Protect credentials; adopt cameras
Assign camera entities to areas
verify: Camera streams in HA.
summary: Bring **UniFi Protect cameras** into HA locally (no Protect subscription needed). The integration pulls live streams, snapshots, and motion/smart-detect events from your Protect controller.

sub: **ha-02 complete.** A local UniFi Protect controller + a dedicated local admin user.

body: **Settings → Devices & Services → Add Integration → UniFi Protect**. Enter the Protect controller IP + a **local** Protect user’s credentials (create a least-privilege local admin in UniFi rather than using your UI.com cloud login).
Adopt the cameras and assign their entities to HA **areas**. You now have local streams + smart-detect event entities for automations.

pitfalls: Using your cloud UI.com account instead of a local Protect user — breaks when cloud auth changes; create a local admin.
Cameras on a VLAN HA can’t reach — allow HA→Protect controller in the firewall.
Expecting AI object zones here — that’s Frigate (ha-15); Protect gives its own smart-detections.
gate: Protect API creds in vault (currently empty)

hardware: Coral TPU (~$60) only if you run Frigate on the Mac mini — the NAS iGPU path instead needs the DS920+ RAM upgrade (Crucial CT16G4SFD8266, ~$40-110)
title: (Optional) Frigate for better local camera AI (zones, package/animal/face)
steps: On your MacBook: open this task's verify block and keep the repo at ~/Documents/Home/foss-setup handy.
Prerequisite: ha-14 complete.
**GATE:** NAS RAM ≥20GB recommended (defer with Immich ML on 4GB).
Prerequisite: DSM **Container Manager** installed (Package Center → Container Manager → Install). Install now if missing.
`scp -r ~/Documents/Home/foss-setup/configs/docker-stack/stacks/frigate nas:/tmp/frigate`
`ssh -t nas 'sudo mkdir -p /volume1/docker/frigate && sudo rsync -a /tmp/frigate/ /volume1/docker/frigate/'`
Copy config/config.yml.example → config.yml; set RTSP URLs + detector (Quick Sync or Coral)
`ssh nas 'cd /volume1/docker/frigate && cp -n .env.example .env && docker compose up -d'`
HA → Frigate integration for person/package events
verify: Frigate detects person; HA receives events.
summary: **(Optional)** Run **Frigate** on the NAS for better local camera AI (object zones, person/package/animal). Pick **one** detector — a **Coral TPU** (recommended) or the NAS **Quick Sync/OpenVINO** iGPU. RAM-gated. Ingest UniFi Protect RTSP substreams; surface events in HA.

sub: **ha-14 complete.** Container Manager installed.
**GATE:** NAS RAM ≥ 20 GB recommended (don’t run alongside Immich ML on 4 GB).
Detector: a **Coral** (~$60, recommended) **or** the NAS J4125 **Quick Sync** iGPU via OpenVINO. (The 2014 Mac mini iGPU is NOT a practical OpenVINO detector — use a Coral on that host.)

body: Copy `config/config.yml.example` → `config.yml`. Enable **RTSP per-camera** in UniFi Protect and paste the `rtsps://` URLs (a LOW-res substream for `detect`, the FULL-res stream for `record`). In the compose, **uncomment the device line** for your detector (`/dev/bus/usb` for Coral USB, `/dev/apex_0` for Coral M.2, or `/dev/dri/renderD128` for Quick Sync) and set the matching detector in `config.yml`. Set `FRIGATE_RTSP_PASSWORD` in `.env`.
detail.note: Image is pinned to `frigate:0.17.1` (0.17.2 was never published). Raise `shm_size` for more/higher-res cameras.

cmds: ssh nas 'cd /volume1/docker/frigate && cp -n .env.example .env && docker compose up -d'
sub: Day-to-day UI is the authenticated `:8971`. Add the **Frigate** integration in HA for person/package event entities + clips.

body: A Coral is the cheapest, lowest-power, best perf/$ option. The NAS iGPU works but is heavier on CPU/GPU. Don’t try to use the old Mac mini iGPU as the detector.

pitfalls: Running on 4 GB next to Immich ML — OOM; wait for the RAM upgrade.
Detecting on the full-res stream — use the low-res substream for `detect`, full-res only for `record`.
Enabling two detectors at once — pick Coral OR Quick Sync, not both.
Expecting the 2014 Mac mini iGPU to do OpenVINO — it can’t; use a Coral there.
gate: NAS sudo password in vault OR DSM GUI

title: Expose HA to Apple Home via the HomeKit Bridge (keep iPhones/Siri)
steps: On your MacBook: open this task's verify block and keep the repo at ~/Documents/Home/foss-setup handy.
Prerequisite: ha-05 complete.
HA → HomeKit Bridge integration
Pair iPhone Home app via QR
Expose only needed domains
verify: Siri controls Hue via HomeKit.
summary: Expose HA devices to **Apple Home** via the **HomeKit Bridge** integration so iPhones/Siri keep working across the whole house (Hue, etc. surfaced into Apple Home). Expose only the domains you actually need.

sub: **ha-05 complete** (some controllable devices, e.g. Hue).

body: **Settings → Devices & Services → Add Integration → HomeKit Bridge**. Choose which **domains/entities** to expose (keep it tight — only what you want in Apple Home).

detail.h: Step 2 — Pair with the iPhone Home app
body: On the iPhone, open **Home → Add Accessory** and scan the **QR/pairing code** HA shows (Settings → Devices → HomeKit Bridge → the pairing notification). Assign to rooms in Apple Home.

detail.note: HomePods ×3 act as the Apple Home hubs; expose a CURATED entity set (lights, climate, vacuums, selected sensors). EXCLUDE the Level locks — they stay paired directly to Apple Home (ha-20); bridging them is impossible anyway (one HomeKit controller per device) and Home Key requires native pairing.

pitfalls: Exposing everything — clutters Apple Home and can hit HomeKit’s accessory limits; expose only needed domains (use multiple bridges if you exceed ~150 accessories).
HA and iPhone on isolated VLANs without mDNS — HomeKit pairing needs mDNS/bonjour reachability.
Missing the pairing code — it’s in the HomeKit Bridge integration’s notification/config.

title: LiteLLM gateway + small always-on fallback model (resilience if the rig is down)
steps: On your MacBook: open this task's verify block and keep the repo at ~/Documents/Home/foss-setup handy.
Prerequisite: docker-01, docker-02 complete.
`scp -r ~/Documents/Home/foss-setup/configs/docker-stack/stacks/litellm mini:/tmp/litellm`
`ssh mini 'sudo mkdir -p /opt/stacks/litellm && sudo rsync -a /tmp/litellm/ /opt/stacks/litellm/'`
`ssh mini 'cd /opt/stacks/litellm && cp -n .env.example .env 2>/dev/null || true'` — LITELLM_MASTER_KEY + Ollama upstream
`ssh mini 'cd /opt/stacks/litellm && docker compose up -d'`
`ssh mini 'docker compose -f /opt/stacks/litellm/compose.yaml ps'`
MacBook browser (Tailscale): http://macmini.<tailnet>:4000
Point HA Assist conversation agent to http://192.168.10.2:4000 (LiteLLM)
On rig: set OLLAMA_KEEP_ALIVE=0 so VRAM frees between requests (game-13)
commands: ssh mini 'cd /opt/stacks/litellm && cp -n .env.example .env && docker compose up -d'
verify: A voice/Assist query answers via LiteLLM (`:4000`); with the rig unreachable (an incident, not routine), the Mac-mini fallback still answers.
summary: Deploy the **LiteLLM** gateway on the Mac mini — one OpenAI-compatible URL (`:4000`) that prefers the rig’s big model and **falls back** to a tiny always-on Mac-mini model if the rig is ever down. The rig is 24/7 (decision 2026-07-08) — the fallback is resilience, and a rig outage is an incident; voice degrades instead of dying while you fix it.

sub: **docker-01, docker-02 complete** (Docker host + `edge` net); **ha-09** for the Assist agent hookup.
Put the generated `sk-...` value in `.env` as `LITELLM_MASTER_KEY` (clients send it as the OpenAI API key).

detail.h: Step 2 — Edit config.yaml (rig primary, mini fallback)
body: Both deployments advertise the same model name `home-llm`; `order:` makes the rig strictly preferred:
detail.note: Two things make the fallback actually work on native Linux Docker: the compose’s `extra_hosts: host.docker.internal:host-gateway`, and the Mac-mini host Ollama listening on all interfaces — set `OLLAMA_HOST=0.0.0.0` and allow TCP 11434. `timeout: 40` avoids false failover during a cold 14B load; `num_retries: 1` fails over fast if the rig is unreachable (an incident — rig is 24/7).

detail.h: Step 4 — Point HA + free rig VRAM
body: Set the HA Assist **conversation agent** base URL to `http://192.168.10.2:4000` (LiteLLM) with the master key. On the rig, set **`OLLAMA_KEEP_ALIVE=0`** so VRAM frees between requests (game-13).

pitfalls: `host.docker.internal` undefined — without the `extra_hosts` host-gateway mapping the Mac-mini fallback never resolves on Linux Docker.
Mac-mini Ollama bound to loopback — set `OLLAMA_HOST=0.0.0.0` or gateway-routed requests are refused.
Putting a >1B fallback on the 8 GB Mac mini — it can’t hold more; keep `llama3.2:1b`.

summary: Deploy **Navidrome** (Subsonic/OpenSubsonic music streaming) on the Mac mini, serving the NAS music library **read-only** over an NFS/SMB mount. Pinned to **0.62.0** (carries auth/IDOR security fixes). Stream to Symfonium/Amperfy/any Subsonic client.

detail.h: Step 1 — Mount the NAS music library (read-only)
cmds: ssh mini 'sudo mkdir -p /mnt/nas/music && sudo mount -t nfs 192.168.10.4:/volume1/music /mnt/nas/music'
ssh mini 'echo "192.168.10.4:/volume1/music /mnt/nas/music nfs ro,_netdev 0 0" | sudo tee -a /etc/fstab'

detail.h: Step 2 — Deploy + set the music path
detail.note: In `.env` set **`MUSIC_FOLDER=/mnt/nas/music`** (the host path, bind-mounted to `/music:ro`). Inside the container `ND_MUSICFOLDER` stays `/music` — don’t point it at the host path. Set PUID/PGID to the owner of `./data`.

sub: Open `http://macmini.<tailnet>:4533`, create the first user, let it scan (`ND_SCANSCHEDULE: 1h`), then stream a track in a Subsonic client.

pitfalls: Setting `ND_MUSICFOLDER` to the host path — that var is the in-container path (`/music`); set the host path via `MUSIC_FOLDER`.
Mounting the music share read-write — keep it `:ro`; the NAS (Lidarr) owns the library.
Running an older Navidrome — 0.62.0 fixes auth-bypass/IDOR issues relevant to a LAN-exposed multi-user install.

detail.h: Step 1 — Make the Tier-1 doc dirs + copy the stack
Step 2 — Adapt the compose for the NAS
body: Remove the external `edge` network (that’s the Mac mini’s Caddy net), bind the consume/media/data dirs at the `/volume1/docs/*` paths if you want them on the docs share, and set PUID/PGID from `id`.
detail.note: Naming quirk (by design): in this compose the **Postgres** service is named `broker` and **Redis** is `redis` — the reverse of upstream. `PAPERLESS_DBHOST=broker` and the pg_dump target are already consistent with that.

sub: SMB-mount the docs share from the MacBook, drop a test PDF into `consume/`, and watch it OCR into a searchable document at `http://192.168.10.4:8000`.

body: Add the bind-mounted `data`/`media`/`consume` dirs to the backup jobs and schedule a **nightly pg_dump** (file-copying a live Postgres dir is not a safe backup):
cmds: ssh nas 'cd /volume1/docker/paperless-ngx && docker compose exec -T broker pg_dump -U paperless paperless | gzip > /volume1/docs/export/paperless-$(date +%F).sql.gz'

pitfalls: Deploying on a 4 GB NAS alongside Immich ML — OOM/thrash. Wait for the 20 GB upgrade.
Backing up only the files, not the DB dump — the search index/metadata lives in Postgres; dump it nightly.
Assuming upstream service names — here `broker`=Postgres, `redis`=Redis (reversed). Use that in any exec/backup command.
Leaving the external `edge` network in the compose on the NAS — it doesn’t exist there; remove it.

verify: rhythmbox detects iPod.
summary: Install the **iPod sync toolchain** on CachyOS — **Rhythmbox + libgpod** — to sync an iPod Classic on Apple’s stock firmware (keeps car/USB-controller integration). The music master is the NAS `/volume1/music`; the iPod is always a reproducible copy.

sub: Installs `rhythmbox` (built-in libgpod-backed iPod support) + `libgpod`. Idempotent (`pacman -S --needed`).
Optional extras: `WITH_GTKPOD=1 …` (power-user GUI for DB surgery); `WITH_HFSPROGS=1 …` only if the iPod was formatted HFS+ on a Mac (AUR build).

detail.note: The iPod **Classic** is USB mass-storage. Do NOT install `usbmuxd`/`ifuse` — those are for iOS / iPod Touch and can confuse detection.

pitfalls: Installing usbmuxd/ifuse for an iPod Classic — wrong stack; it’s plain USB mass storage.
Building hfsprogs when the iPod is already FAT32 — only HFS+ (Mac-formatted) iPods need it.

hardware: iPod Classic (plus an optional flash/SD storage mod)
title: Sync the iPod Classic from CachyOS via Rhythmbox/libgpod
steps: Prerequisite: read-10, nas-00d (NFS), and nas-23 (Lidarr → /volume1/music) complete.
On the rig: mount the NAS music master — `sudo mkdir -p /mnt/nas/music`; fstab entry `192.168.10.4:/volume1/music /mnt/nas/music nfs defaults,_netdev 0 0`; `sudo mount -a`.
Rhythmbox → Preferences → Music → add /mnt/nas/music as a library location. Do NOT rename Lidarr's /music layout without checking both Plex and this sync.
Plug in the iPod; Rhythmbox auto-detects it. Sync selected playlists/tracks to the device.
Eject from Rhythmbox before unplugging. See scripts/media/ipod-sync-cachyos.md for FirewireGuid/SysInfoExtended first-time setup.
verify: A playlist synced from /mnt/nas/music (NAS /volume1/music) plays on the iPod.
summary: Sync the **iPod Classic** from CachyOS via Rhythmbox/libgpod, pulling from the NAS music master at `/volume1/music`. Handle the re-init gotchas (FirewireGuid/SysInfoExtended) so the iTunesDB writes cleanly.

sub: **read-10 complete** (tools), plus **nas-00d** and **nas-23** so `/volume1/music` is populated by Lidarr.

body: Plug in and find the partition/filesystem; desktops usually auto-mount under `/run/media/$USER/…`:
sub: `vfat` = FAT32 (Windows-initialized, easiest on Linux). `hfsplus` = HFS+ (Mac-initialized; needs `hfsprogs` and a `force,rw` mount).

body: If the iPod was never initialized by libgpod, the iTunesDB write can fail until libgpod knows the device’s FirewireGuid. Follow the ArchWiki iPod page to generate `SysInfoExtended` (libgpod’s `ipod-read-sysinfo-extended`) into the iPod’s `iPod_Control/Device/` so syncs write correctly.

detail.h: Step 3 — Mount the music master + sync
body: Access the NAS music master (`/volume1/music`, e.g. via SMB/NFS mount on the rig). In Rhythmbox, the iPod appears as a device; create/sync playlists from the master. The iPod is disposable — if the DB ever gets confused, wipe and re-sync from the master.

pitfalls: Skipping SysInfoExtended on a fresh iPod — libgpod can’t write a valid iTunesDB and the iPod shows ‘0 songs’.
Treating the iPod as the master — it’s a reproducible copy of `/volume1/music`; never the source of truth.
HFS+ mounted read-only — needs `force,rw` (and hfsprogs) or syncs fail.

summary: Deploy **Immich** on the DS920+ with Container Manager, pinned to a known release, with **Quick Sync** video transcoding and **OpenVINO** ML inference via `/dev/dri` passthrough. Originals live in `UPLOAD_LOCATION=/volume1/photo` (Tier 1, irreplaceable) and Postgres in `/volume1/docker/immich/postgres`, both protected by a nightly `pg_dump` + Hyper Backup.

sub: **nas-01, nas-00c, nas-prep-01 complete** (volumes/shares ready; `/volume1/photo` exists).
DSM **Container Manager** installed (Package Center → Container Manager → Install).
Confirm the iGPU device node exists (needed for Quick Sync + OpenVINO):
detail.note: If `/dev/dri` is **missing**, remove the `devices:` blocks from both `immich-server` and `immich-machine-learning` before deploying, or transcoding/ML will fail to start.

detail.h: Step 1 — Copy the stack to the NAS

detail.note: The Postgres image is the **VectorChord** build pinned by digest — never swap it for a generic `postgres:release`; Immich major Postgres upgrades are not automatic. Redis is pinned to a Valkey digest matching the release.

cmds: ssh nas 'cd /volume1/docker/immich && docker compose pull && docker compose up -d'
sub: Four containers come up: `immich_server`, `immich_machine_learning` (`-openvino` variant), `immich_redis`, `immich_postgres`. First start runs DB migrations — give it a minute.

body: Browse to `http://192.168.10.4:2283` → create the **admin** account. Then **Administration → Settings → Video Transcoding → Hardware Acceleration → Quick Sync**. (Smart-search/ML uses the OpenVINO container automatically.)

Immich’s own guidance: back up the **database dump** plus `UPLOAD_LOCATION`. Add a DSM Task Scheduler nightly job that dumps Postgres to `/volume1/docker/immich/backups`, then include `/volume1/photo` + the backups dir in **Hyper Backup Tier 1**:
cmds: ssh nas 'docker exec -t immich_postgres pg_dumpall --clean --if-exists -U postgres | gzip > /volume1/docker/immich/backups/immich-$(date +%F).sql.gz'
detail.note: Schedule the dump (DSM Task Scheduler, User = root, daily) and only add the share to Hyper Backup after the first dump succeeds.

Audit 2026-07-07: Immich itself is healthy on NAS :2283, but the pg_dump job was never scheduled in DSM Task Scheduler (only a single manual dump from 2026-07-02 exists), and a duplicate Immich on the mini is crash-looping (decommission = fix-12). Reopened until the dump schedule runs on its own. Scheduling needs NAS sudo/DSM GUI — see gate.

pitfalls: Deploying with the `devices: /dev/dri` blocks when `/dev/dri` doesn’t exist — containers fail. Check first.
Swapping the VectorChord Postgres image for a stock Postgres — breaks Immich’s vector search and risks an unsupported major upgrade.
Starting before filling `.env` — a weak/blank `DB_PASSWORD` bakes into the DB volume.
Backing up only the photo files, not the DB dump — you can’t restore the library/faces/albums without the Postgres dump.
gate: NAS sudo password in vault OR DSM GUI
verified: Re-verified 2026-07-07: nightly pg_dump in root crontab, fresh dump verified (16.7MB, gzip -t ok); duplicate mini Immich stopped.
