summary: Import a **mirrorless-camera SD card** into Immich using **immich-go** from the CachyOS rig. A camera card has no auto-backup, so the flow is **SD → SSD (safety copy) → Immich**; immich-go stacks RAW+JPEG pairs as one asset and dedups by checksum, so re-runs are safe.

sub: **nas-08 complete** (Immich up) and **net-14** (rig has network/route to the NAS).
immich-go installed on the rig (single static Go binary — see `immich-go-import.md`).

detail.h: Step 1 — Create an Immich API key
body: Immich web UI → **Account Settings → API Keys → New API Key**, name it `immich-go`, copy the value (shown once). Treat it like a password.

detail.h: Step 2 — Copy the card OFF first (SD → SSD)
body: Copy everything to a **dated** folder on the rig SSD before touching Immich or reformatting the card — this is your originals safety copy:

Always dry-run first to see what would upload, then run for real. The script calls `immich-go upload from-folder --manage-raw-jpeg=StackCoverRaw --manage-burst=Stack --recursive` (verified current syntax):
detail.note: immich-go has **no `--watch`** flag (that’s the official Immich CLI, a different tool). For continuous ingest, re-run this script on a cron/timer — it’s idempotent (server-side checksum dedup).

detail.h: Step 5 — Keep the SSD copy until verified
body: Confirm the RAW+JPEG appear **stacked** in Immich, then keep the SSD copy until Immich itself is backed up (its `UPLOAD_LOCATION` is Tier 1). Only then reformat the card.

pitfalls: Importing straight from the live card and reformatting before verifying — copy to SSD first; that copy is your only safety net.
Expecting `--watch` on immich-go — use a cron/timer re-run, or the official `immich upload --watch` (no RAW stacking).
Wrong `--manage-raw-jpeg` mode — `StackCoverRaw` keeps RAW as cover (edit-friendly); use `StackCoverJPG` if you’d rather the JPEG be primary.
Pointing `IMMICH_SERVER` at the wrong scheme — use `http://192.168.10.4:2283` on LAN or your Caddy HTTPS URL.

Verified safe: Hyper Backup uses application key 'tabaskakey' (0056e2...0001), NOT the master, so regeneration breaks nothing. Optional hardening while in the console: restrict/recreate tabaskakey scoped to bucket-hyper-backup only (it needs write+delete there for Smart Recycle; today its scope is broader, though bucket-restic is shielded by GOVERNANCE retention regardless). Bucket admin afterwards = scripts/backup/b2-apply-bucket-policy.py with B2_MASTER_KEY_ID/B2_MASTER_KEY passed via env.
steps: B2 console -> Account -> Application Keys -> regenerate master application key

Confirm daily checks stay green: b2-restic-immutable, b2-bucket-policy, nas-hyperbackup-b2-fresh
verify: New master key works with b2-apply-bucket-policy.py (no --backfill needed); old value dead; HB nightly still succeeds; verification sweep green.

title: ntfy least-privilege: per-publisher users/tokens instead of everything on admin
summary: Follow-through on fix-23 (2026-07-17): all 8 ntfy tokens (nas-health, beszel, diun, diun-nas, rig-aer, healthchecks, uptime-kuma, verification) hang off the ADMIN user, so any single leaked token is read-write to every topic — the leaked nas-health token proved this class. Create one ntfy user per publisher with write access to only its topic(s), mint tokens under those users, swap each consumer (NAS health.env, diun envs on mini+nas, beszel, rig pcie-aer env, /etc/verification/env, uptime-kuma, healthchecks), revoke the admin-owned tokens, update the vault ntfy.* keys. Consumer-test each publish path after its swap (the fix-23 rotation cookbook in wiki/runbooks/secrets-hygiene.md). Keep ntfy-anon-publish-denied green throughout.
steps: Follow the /resolve-finding definition of done; one consumer at a time, publish-test each before revoking its old token.
verify: ntfy token list shows no publisher tokens on admin; each consumer publish-tested 200; vault updated; ntfy-anon-publish-denied still green.

title: Rotate credentials that left the fleet: Plex token (iCloud snapshot) + secrets printed in the committed audit doc; purge iCloud Recently Deleted
summary: Follow-through on fix-23 (2026-07-17): deleting the exposure is not rotating what was exposed. (1) The migration-snapshot's Plex Preferences.xml (PlexOnlineToken) replicated to Apple's iCloud for ~3 weeks — rotate it (Plex account 'sign out connected devices' regenerates server tokens; brief re-auth on clients, do in the 4-7AM window) and update vault plex.token. (2) The committed quality-gate doc printed at least the whisparr API key (M26 evidence) and named the old ntfy token (already revoked by fix-23) — sweep quality-gate-2026-07-16.{md,json} for every other printed credential, regenerate each in its app, update vault + consumers (prowlarr sync, unpackerr, etc.), and redact the values from the doc. (3) OPERATOR: purge 'Recently Deleted' in iCloud Drive/Finder so the 15GB snapshot's server-side copies die now instead of in ~30 days.
steps: Follow the /resolve-finding definition of done; gate on the user for the Plex sign-out step and the iCloud purge.
verify: Old Plex token rejected by plex.tv API; grep of the audit doc finds no live credential values; rotated keys work end-to-end from their consumers; iCloud Recently Deleted purged (operator confirms).

title: Fix set -e in self-healing scripts (failures silently ignored)
summary: Both self-healing scripts run with `set -uo pipefail` — the missing `-e` means a failed rclone remount or docker restart is silently swallowed and the script still exits 0, defeating the whole point of a watchdog.
steps: Edit scripts/media/rclone-seedbox-watchdog.sh line 25: change `set -uo pipefail` to `set -euo pipefail`.
Edit scripts/nas/nas-docker-health.sh line 9: same change.
Audit both scripts for commands that are ALLOWED to fail (grep probes, optional curls) and guard them explicitly with `|| true` so -e doesn't turn probes into aborts.
Redeploy both scripts to the NAS over SSH (same path DSM Task Scheduler already calls) and run each once manually.
Inject a failure (e.g. temporarily rename the rclone remote) and confirm the script now exits non-zero and the ntfy alert fires.

title: Fix $COMPOSE word-splitting (convert to bash array)

steps: In both scripts replace `COMPOSE="docker compose"` with `COMPOSE=(docker compose)`.
Replace every `$COMPOSE ...` call site with `"${COMPOSE[@]}" ...`.
Run shellcheck over both; fix any remaining SC2086 hits introduced by the same pattern.
Redeploy to their target hosts and run once manually.

title: Fix install-slskd-native.sh default path ($HOME/$HOME bug)
summary: Line 17 builds the install dir by prefixing an already-absolute `$HOME` with `$HOME/` — the default resolves to `/home/user/home/user/…`, so the script fails on the very seedbox it targets.
steps: Fix line 17 so the default is a single absolute path, e.g. `INSTALL_DIR="${1:-$HOME/slskd}"` — never concatenate $HOME onto an absolute segment.
Add an early `echo "installing to: $INSTALL_DIR"` so a wrong path is obvious in dry runs.
Sanity-check with `bash -n` and a dry run on any Linux host.
Live re-run on Betty is blocked until the Tailscale SSH ACL for operator → seedbox is opened (see the 2026-07-07 audit) — slskd is already installed there, so this is a repo correctness fix.
verify: Dry run prints a single sane absolute path; no `$HOME/$HOME` concatenation remains.

title: Idempotency guard for /etc/fuse.conf append in rclone mount script
summary: scripts/media/rclone-seedbox-mount.sh:101 appends `user_allow_other` to /etc/fuse.conf unconditionally — every re-run adds another line. Harmless today, but it's config drift and masks whether the script has run before.
steps: Wrap the append: `grep -qx 'user_allow_other' /etc/fuse.conf || echo user_allow_other | sudo tee -a /etc/fuse.conf`.
Check the NAS's current /etc/fuse.conf and de-duplicate any existing repeats.
Re-run the script twice and diff /etc/fuse.conf between runs.
verify: Running the mount script twice leaves exactly one `user_allow_other` line in /etc/fuse.conf.

title: Repair ansible-pull convergence (12/12 runs failing on mini)

Remove `--check` from the ansible-pull ExecStart — this was a dry-run left in place; delete it deliberately and note the change in the unit comment.

summary: Ship it: a dockerized mkdocs build produces the static site, Caddy serves it at wiki.tabaska.us via file_server (no always-on container needed), and Homepage links it. Rebuild-on-push comes free once the repo publish flow (fix-07) runs the build.
steps: Build step: `docker run --rm -v wiki:/docs squidfunk/mkdocs-material build` → site/ output synced to /opt/stacks/wiki-site on the mini.
Caddy vhost: `wiki.tabaska.us { root * /srv/wiki; file_server }` with the site dir mounted into the Caddy container.
AdGuard rewrite for wiki.tabaska.us → 192.168.10.2 (both instances).
Add a small deploy script scripts/wiki/deploy-wiki.sh (build + rsync + no reload needed).
Homepage entry (home-04 links it prominently).
verify: https://wiki.tabaska.us serves the built site with working search and nav.

title: Wiki-in-the-loop — every change updates the wiki in the same commit
summary: The flow rule that keeps the manual alive: any change to a service, script, or host updates the relevant wiki page in the same commit. Enforced two ways — the generators make service/script/host pages automatic, and a drift check fails when generated pages are stale.
steps: Write the rule into the root README and the agent flow notes: config change ⇒ regenerate wiki (make wiki) ⇒ commit together.
Drift check: a CI-ish script (runnable by verify-02's timer) that regenerates into a temp dir and diffs against committed pages; non-empty diff → ntfy alert.
Add the check to the verification probe library (verify-01) as check `wiki-drift`.
Prove it: make a trivial compose change, watch the drift alert, fix by regenerating.
verify: A deliberate stale-wiki commit triggers the drift alert; regenerating clears it.

title: Probe library — checks.d/*.yaml per service and host
summary: The verification backbone: a declarative probe library at configs/verification/checks.d/ — one YAML per service/host with the probe command, expected result, severity, and a runbook link. Everything the fleet audit found by hand becomes a repeatable check.
steps: Define the check schema: id, host, cmd (run over SSH), expect (exit code / regex / json path), severity (page / warn / info), runbook (wiki link), interval.

Write checks from the audit's failure catalog: dns (dig against mini + NAS + rewrites), docker (no crash-looping containers, restart counts stable), backups (last snapshot/dump age), git-hygiene (dirty /opt/stacks, etckeeper alive, subtree in sync), storage (mounts present, fstab clean), systemd (--failed empty), GPU cap (rig), ansible-pull freshness.
One YAML per concern, kept small — the runner (verify-02) globs the directory.
Each check names its runbook page (wiki-03) so alerts are actionable.
verify: checks.d covers every audit finding class; each check has cmd/expect/severity/runbook and validates against the schema.

title: Runner + schedule — run-checks over SSH from mini, ntfy on regression
summary: The engine: scripts/verification/run-checks executes every checks.d probe over SSH from the mini, writes timestamped JSON results, runs on a systemd timer, and alerts via ntfy ONLY on regression (pass→fail transition) so alerts stay meaningful.
steps: run-checks: glob checks.d, execute each cmd on its host over SSH (BatchMode, timeout), evaluate expect, emit results JSON (check id, host, status, output, duration, ts).
State diffing: compare against the previous results file; regression (pass→fail) and recovery (fail→pass) produce ntfy notifications with the runbook link; steady-state stays silent.
systemd service+timer on the mini (e.g. every 30 min; per-check interval respected).
Results retained under /var/lib/verification/ (and latest.json symlink) for verify-03 triage + the wiki status page.
Dry-run the full suite once and fix flaky probes before enabling the timer.
verify: Timer runs the suite; latest.json shows per-check status; killing a test container produces exactly one ntfy regression alert with a runbook link.

title: Local-LLM triage skills — scoped prompts, one failure per context
summary: When a check fails, a LOCAL model on the rig triages it: small scoped skills (dns, docker, backups, git-hygiene, storage) — each a tight prompt with the check's output + relevant context, one failure per fresh context window, strict structured output (JSON verdict: cause hypothesis, safe next command, runbook section). Multiple small agents, never one big one.
steps: Skill format: configs/verification/skills/<domain>.md — role, allowed evidence, output JSON schema {hypothesis, confidence, next_safe_command, runbook_anchor}, and hard rules (no destructive commands, cite evidence lines).
Write the five domain skills: dns, docker, backups, git-hygiene, storage — each grounded in the matching runbook.
Triage driver: for each NEW failure, one fresh LiteLLM call (fresh context — no accumulation across failures) routing to the rig model (verify-04); attach skill + check output + last-known-good.
Validate output against the JSON schema; malformed → retry once → fall back to raw alert.
Append the triage verdict to the ntfy alert so the notification arrives pre-diagnosed.
verify: A seeded dns failure produces a schema-valid triage verdict from the local model appended to the alert; each failure demonstrably ran in its own context window.

title: Rig model setup — pin models sized for the 3090 Ti, LiteLLM routes
summary: Provision the triage brain: pull and pin Ollama models sized for the 3090 Ti's 24 GB (qwen2.5-coder:14b and 32b-q4 for triage, llama3.1:8b as the fast/fallback lane), add LiteLLM routes for them, and wire auth so the mini's triage driver can call the gateway.
steps: On the rig: `ollama pull qwen2.5-coder:14b`, `ollama pull qwen2.5-coder:32b-instruct-q4_K_M`, `ollama pull llama3.1:8b` — pin exact tags in configs.
Sanity: 32b-q4 fits 24 GB VRAM with headroom; verify with `ollama ps` + nvidia-smi during a test generation; keep `keep_alive` modest so games reclaim VRAM.
LiteLLM (mini): add model routes triage-big → rig 32b-q4, triage-fast → llama3.1:8b, with the existing fallback chain if the rig is down (resilience).
Auth: LiteLLM virtual key for the verification runner, stored in the local vault; triage driver reads it from env.
End-to-end: one test completion through LiteLLM from the mini hitting the rig.
verify: All three models pinned and listed; a completion via LiteLLM's triage route returns from the rig; VRAM fits with headroom.

title: Regression wiring — failed checks auto-reopen tasks in progress.json
summary: Close the loop with the tracker: each check maps to the task id(s) it proves. On a confirmed regression, the runner edits docs/progress.json — removes the id from `done` and records the reason under `reopened` — and commits, so this page shows reality without anyone remembering to update it.
steps: Add `proves: [task-id, …]` to the check schema and annotate existing checks (e.g. dns NAS probe proves dns-02; GPU cap proves game-10; immich dump age proves nas-08).

Runner hook: on regression of a check with `proves`, update docs/progress.json (remove from done, add reopened reason with date + check id) in the repo clone, commit, push to Forgejo, publish (fix-07 flow).
Guard rails: only on confirmed regression (two consecutive fails), never auto-close (humans/AI runs close tasks), and the commit message names the check.
Recovery does NOT auto-mark done — it sends a 'ready to re-verify' ntfy instead.
Test with a seeded failure end-to-end: check fails twice → progress.json commit appears → page shows the task reopened.
verify: Seeded regression produces a progress.json commit reopening the mapped task with reason; page reflects it on next load; no auto-close path exists.

Staggered-versioning on the NAS side so a bad sync never destroys a save.
Devices stay playable offline; sync happens when they see the tailnet/LAN.

title: Indexer redundancy layer in Prowlarr (TV/anime over IPT+MAM)
steps: Goal: remove the IPT-only single point of failure for TV/anime search (the 'IPT ~0 grabs' gap) by adding indexer redundancy in Prowlarr.

Resolution (operator decision, 2026-07-14): satisfied with PRIVATE-tracker redundancy instead of public indexers. Prowlarr now carries IPTorrents + OnlyEncodes+ + RetroToon + SexTorrent + Zenith (+ MyAnonamouse for books, BitPorn) and fullSyncs its TV-capable indexers to Sonarr/Radarr/Readarr/Lidarr.
Public piracy indexers (EZTV / The Pirate Bay / Nyaa / SubsPlease) were DECLINED and are NOT added.

Verified: 5 TV-capable indexers synced to Sonarr with automatic-search enabled; Prowlarr indexerstatus shows no failing indexers; all four *arr apps at fullSync. Guarded by the sonarr-indexer-redundancy check (>=3 search-enabled indexers).

title: Deploy Jellyfin as a fully-local media server (plex.tv-independent parallel to Plex)
steps: Motivation: Plex is cloud-tethered — auth + server discovery route through plex.tv, so a plex.tv outage locks out even LAN clients (hit 2026-07-14). Jellyfin is FOSS + FULLY LOCAL: local accounts, LAN discovery, no external dependency ever.

Deploy Jellyfin (jellyfin/jellyfin, pinned tag) as a NAS container via Container Manager on the shared 'edge' pattern; bind the SAME NAS media read-only: /volume1/movies, /volume2 or the tv share, /volume1/music, /volume1/youtube. Do NOT touch/remove Plex — run in parallel.
Enable HW transcoding via the DS920+ QuickSync iGPU (VA-API /dev/dri) if the Celeron supports it; else direct-play only. Create local users; NO cloud sign-in.
Add a Caddy vhost jellyfin.tabaska.us (import local_tls; reverse_proxy {$NAS_IP}:8096) per the add-a-service runbook (compose + catalog + homepage tile + wiki page + Uptime-Kuma + coverage manifest).
Install native Jellyfin apps (Apple TV/iOS via Swiftfin, Android, web) and confirm playback.
Add a checks.d/ acceptance check mirroring the Plex ones: Jellyfin serves its libraries + a Radarr/Sonarr-vs-Jellyfin coverage probe (arr-plex-journey.py pattern, adapted to the Jellyfin API).
verify: jellyfin.tabaska.us serves Movies/TV/Music; a local client plays a title with the NAS's WAN access BLOCKED (proves zero cloud dependency).

title: Resolve the rig ~/Music mirror conflict (05:00 ALAC transcode vs 05:30 rsync --delete-after)
summary: Two rig systemd timers both target ~/Music and fight: nas-music-mirror.service (05:00) transcodes FLAC->ALAC into ~/Music, then music-mirror.service (05:30) runs `rsync -rt --delete-after` and deletes the .m4a files the transcode just created (they don't exist on the NAS source). Both are enabled+active as of 2026-07-14 — a nightly data-loss + wasted-CPU loop. Pick ONE authoritative mirror for ~/Music (or point them at separate target dirs / drop --delete-after).
steps: Decide which mirror owns ~/Music: (a) keep the ALAC transcode (nas-music-mirror) and retire/redirect the verbatim rsync, (b) keep the verbatim rsync and retire the transcode, or (c) give each its own target dir so neither deletes the other.
Edit the units in `foss-setup/configs/host/rig/music-mirror/` to match (change WorkingDirectory/target, disable a timer, or remove `--delete-after`).
Deploy to the rig and reconcile: `ssh rig 'sudo systemctl daemon-reload'` then enable/disable the timers per the decision.
Verify no cross-deletion after a nightly run (or trigger both): the ALAC .m4a files survive.
verify: After a nightly cycle, ~/Music still contains the ALAC .m4a transcodes (the 05:30 rsync no longer deletes them); exactly one timer owns ~/Music (or they target separate dirs). Surfaced by the rig-music-mirror-conflict finding 2026-07-14.

title: MusicSeerr can still create unmonitored-artist requests (upstream monitor_artist=0 default) — tripwire-covered, close the generator
summary: Follow-through on fix-25 (2026-07-17, H14/M13): the 3OH!3 phantom happened because the musicseerr request carried monitor_artist=0, leaving the Lidarr artist unmonitored so the album was excluded from wanted/missing and never retried. fix-25 fixed the DATA (artist monitored, album imported) and added the arr-orphan-monitor-flags tripwire that catches any recurrence within the hour — but the GENERATOR remains: musicseerr v1.4.2 (upstream image ghcr.io/habirabbu/musicseerr, no bind-mounted code to patch) can still create such requests. Options: config/UI default forcing monitor-artist on requests if the app supports it, an upstream issue/PR, or a small reconciler that monitors the artist whenever a request enters 'downloading'. Until then the tripwire (warn severity, checks.d/media.yaml) is the guard.
steps: Check musicseerr settings/UI + upstream repo for a monitor-artist default; prefer config over patch.
If no upstream knob: decide reconciler vs upstream PR; implement + codify in the stack mirror.
Keep arr-orphan-monitor-flags as the regression tripwire; verify with a test request.
verify: A new musicseerr request for an album by an unmonitored artist ends up with the artist monitored in Lidarr (or the request is refused), and arr-orphan-monitor-flags stays green.
